**Edinburgh**[[a]](#cite_note-6) is the [capital city](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") of [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and one of its 32 [council areas](/wiki/Council_areas_of_Scotland "Council areas of Scotland"). It is located in southeast Scotland and is bounded to the north by the [Firth of Forth](/wiki/Firth_of_Forth "Firth of Forth") and to the south by the [Pentland Hills](/wiki/Pentland_Hills "Pentland Hills"). Edinburgh had a population of
506,520 in 2020,[[6]](#cite_note-NRS_Locality_Estimate-7) making it the [second-most-populous](/wiki/List_of_towns_and_cities_in_Scotland_by_population "List of towns and cities in Scotland by population") city in Scotland and the [seventh-most-populous](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of cities in the United Kingdom") in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). The [wider metropolitan area](/wiki/Functional_urban_area "Functional urban area") had a population of 912,490 in the same year.[[7]](#cite_note-8)

Recognised as the capital of Scotland since at least the 15th century, Edinburgh is the seat of the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government"), the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament"), the [highest courts in Scotland](/wiki/Courts_of_Scotland "Courts of Scotland"), and the [Palace of Holyroodhouse](/wiki/Palace_of_Holyroodhouse "Palace of Holyroodhouse"), the [official residence](/wiki/Official_residence "Official residence") of the [British monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") in Scotland. It is also the annual venue of the [General Assembly of the Church of Scotland](/wiki/General_Assembly_of_the_Church_of_Scotland "General Assembly of the Church of Scotland"). The city has long been a centre of education, particularly in the fields of medicine, [Scottish law](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law"), [literature](/wiki/Scottish_literature "Scottish literature"), [philosophy](/wiki/Scottish_philosophy "Scottish philosophy"), the sciences and engineering. The [University of Edinburgh](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh "University of Edinburgh") was founded in 1582 and is now one of three universities in the city. The [financial centre](/wiki/Financial_centre "Financial centre") of Scotland, Edinburgh was ranked as the second-largest financial centre in the United Kingdom, the fourth-largest in Europe, and the thirteenth-largest in the world in the [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index") in 2020.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

The city is a cultural centre, and is the home of institutions including the [National Museum of Scotland](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Scotland "National Museum of Scotland"), the [National Library of Scotland](/wiki/National_Library_of_Scotland "National Library of Scotland"), and the [Scottish National Gallery](/wiki/Scottish_National_Gallery "Scottish National Gallery").[[9]](#cite_note-10) The city is also known for the [Edinburgh International Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_International_Festival "Edinburgh International Festival") and [the Fringe](/wiki/Edinburgh_Festival_Fringe "Edinburgh Festival Fringe"), the latter being the world's largest annual international arts festival. Historic sites in Edinburgh include [Edinburgh Castle](/wiki/Edinburgh_Castle "Edinburgh Castle"), the Palace of Holyroodhouse, [St Giles' Cathedral](/wiki/St_Giles%27_Cathedral "St Giles' Cathedral"), [Greyfriars Kirk](/wiki/Greyfriars_Kirk "Greyfriars Kirk"), [Canongate Kirk](/wiki/Canongate_Kirk "Canongate Kirk") and the extensive Georgian New Town built in the 18th and 19th centuries. The [Old Town](/wiki/Old_Town,_Edinburgh "Old Town, Edinburgh") and the [New Town](/wiki/New_Town,_Edinburgh "New Town, Edinburgh") are together listed as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO"),[[10]](#cite_note-www.edinburgh.org_world-heritage-site-11) and the site has been managed by [Edinburgh World Heritage](/wiki/Edinburgh_World_Heritage "Edinburgh World Heritage") since 1999. The city's historical and cultural attractions have made it Britain's second-most-visited tourist destination, attracting 5.3 million visits, including 2.4 million from overseas, in 2023.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

Edinburgh is governed by the [City of Edinburgh Council](/wiki/City_of_Edinburgh_Council "City of Edinburgh Council"), a unitary authority. The City of Edinburgh council area had an estimated population of 530,680 in 2024,[[2]](#cite_note-popstats-2) and includes outlying towns and villages. The city is in the [Lothian](/wiki/Lothian "Lothian") region and was historically part of the [shire](/wiki/Shires_of_Scotland "Shires of Scotland") of [Midlothian](/wiki/Midlothian "Midlothian") (also called Edinburghshire).

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

Main article: [Etymology of Edinburgh](/wiki/Etymology_of_Edinburgh "Etymology of Edinburgh")

"Edin", the root of the city's name, derives from **[Eidyn](/wiki/Eidyn "Eidyn")**, the name for the region in [Cumbric](/wiki/Cumbric "Cumbric"), the [Brittonic](/wiki/Brittonic_languages "Brittonic languages") [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") language formerly spoken there. The name's meaning is unknown.[[12]](#cite_note-Gelling-13) The district of Eidyn was centred on the stronghold of Din Eidyn, the [dun](/wiki/Dun_(fortification) "Dun (fortification)") or [hillfort](/wiki/Hillfort "Hillfort") of Eidyn.[[12]](#cite_note-Gelling-13) This stronghold is believed to have been located at [Castle Rock](/wiki/Castle_Rock,_Edinburgh "Castle Rock, Edinburgh"),[[13]](#cite_note-14) now the site of [Edinburgh Castle](/wiki/Edinburgh_Castle "Edinburgh Castle"). A siege of Din Eidyn by [Oswald](/wiki/Oswald_of_Northumbria "Oswald of Northumbria"), king of the [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)") of [Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria") in 638 marked the beginning of three centuries of Germanic influence in south east Scotland that laid the foundations for the development of [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language"), before the town was ultimately subsumed in 954 by the kingdom known to the English as Scotland.[[14]](#cite_note-Driscoll229-15) As the language shifted from Cumbric to [Northumbrian Old English](/wiki/Northumbrian_Old_English "Northumbrian Old English") and then [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language"), the Brittonic *din* in Din Eidyn was replaced by *[burh](/wiki/Burh "Burh")*, producing *Edinburgh*. In [Scottish Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic "Scottish Gaelic") *din* becomes *dùn*, producing modern *Dùn Èideann*.[[12]](#cite_note-Gelling-13)[[15]](#cite_note-16)

## Nicknames

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Nicknames")]

The city is nicknamed ***Auld Reekie***,[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) Scots for *Old Smoky*, for the views from the country of the smoke-covered Old Town. A note in a collection of the works of the poet [Allan Ramsay](/wiki/Allan_Ramsay_(poet) "Allan Ramsay (poet)") explains, "Auld Reeky...A name the country people give Edinburgh, from the cloud of smoke or reek that is always impending over it."[[18]](#cite_note-19) In [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott "Walter Scott")'s 1820 novel *[The Abbot](/wiki/The_Abbot "The Abbot")*, a character observes that "yonder stands Auld Reekie—you may see the smoke hover over her at twenty miles' distance".[[19]](#cite_note-20) [Thomas Carlyle](/wiki/Thomas_Carlyle "Thomas Carlyle") comments on the phenomenon: "Smoke cloud hangs over old Edinburgh, for, ever since [Aeneas Silvius](/wiki/Pope_Pius_II "Pope Pius II")'s time and earlier, the people have the art, very strange to Aeneas, of burning a certain sort of black stones, and Edinburgh with its chimneys is called 'Auld Reekie' by the country people".[[20]](#cite_note-21) The 19th-century historian [Robert Chambers](/wiki/Robert_Chambers_(publisher_born_1802) "Robert Chambers (publisher born 1802)") asserted that the [sobriquet](/wiki/Sobriquet "Sobriquet") could not be traced before the reign of [King Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") in the late 17th century. He attributed the name to a [Fife](/wiki/Fife "Fife") laird, Durham of Largo, who regulated the bedtime of his children by the smoke rising above Edinburgh from the fires of the tenements. "It's time now bairns, to tak' the beuks, and gang to our beds, for yonder's Auld Reekie, I see, putting on her nicht-cap!".[[21]](#cite_note-22)

Edinburgh has been popularly called the *Athens of the North* since the early 19th century.[[22]](#cite_note-Lowrey-23) References to [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens"), such as *Athens of Britain* and *Modern Athens*, had been made as early as the 1760s. The similarities were seen to be topographical but also intellectual. Edinburgh's Castle Rock reminded returning [grand tourists](/wiki/Grand_Tour "Grand Tour") of the [Athenian](/wiki/Athens "Athens") [Acropolis](/wiki/Acropolis,_Athens "Acropolis, Athens"), as did aspects of the [neoclassical architecture](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") and layout of [New Town](/wiki/New_Town,_Edinburgh "New Town, Edinburgh").[[22]](#cite_note-Lowrey-23) In 1818, naturalist [Edward Daniel Clarke](/wiki/Edward_Daniel_Clarke "Edward Daniel Clarke") called Edinburgh "a very correct model of a Grecian city", pointing out perceived similarities between both cities and their [ports](/wiki/Port "Port") (respectively, [Leith](/wiki/Leith "Leith") and [Piraeus](/wiki/Piraeus "Piraeus")).[[22]](#cite_note-Lowrey-23) Intellectually, the [Scottish Enlightenment](/wiki/Scottish_Enlightenment "Scottish Enlightenment"), with its [humanist](/wiki/Humanism "Humanism") and [rationalist](/wiki/Rationalism "Rationalism") outlook, was influenced by [Ancient Greek philosophy](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_philosophy "Ancient Greek philosophy").[[23]](#cite_note-24) In 1822 the Scottish landscape painter [Hugh William Williams](/wiki/Hugh_William_Williams "Hugh William Williams") organised an exhibition that showed his paintings of Athens alongside views of Edinburgh, and the idea of a direct parallel between both cities quickly caught the popular imagination.[[24]](#cite_note-25) When plans were drawn up in the early 19th century to architecturally develop [Calton Hill](/wiki/Calton_Hill "Calton Hill"), the design of the [National Monument](/wiki/National_Monument_of_Scotland "National Monument of Scotland") directly copied Athens' [Parthenon](/wiki/Parthenon "Parthenon").[[25]](#cite_note-McKee-26) [Tom Stoppard](/wiki/Tom_Stoppard "Tom Stoppard")'s character Archie of *[Jumpers](/wiki/Jumpers_(play) "Jumpers (play)")* said, perhaps playing on [Reykjavík](/wiki/Reykjav%C3%ADk "Reykjavík") meaning "smoky bay", that the "Reykjavík of the South" would be more appropriate.[[26]](#cite_note-27)

The city has also been known by several [Latin names](/wiki/List_of_Latin_place_names_in_Britain#Cities_and_towns_in_Scotland "List of Latin place names in Britain"), such as *Edinburgum*, while the adjectival forms *Edinburgensis* and *Edinensis* are used in educational and scientific contexts.[[27]](#cite_note-Grässe1909-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)

*Edina* is a late 18th-century poetical form used by the Scottish poets [Robert Fergusson](/wiki/Robert_Fergusson "Robert Fergusson") and [Robert Burns](/wiki/Robert_Burns "Robert Burns"). "Embra" or "Embro" are colloquialisms from the same time,[[29]](#cite_note-30) as in [Robert Garioch](/wiki/Robert_Garioch "Robert Garioch")'s *Embro to the Ploy*.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

[Ben Jonson](/wiki/Ben_Jonson "Ben Jonson") described it as "Britaine's other eye",[[31]](#cite_note-32) and Sir Walter Scott referred to it as "yon Empress of the North".[[32]](#cite_note-33) [Robert Louis Stevenson](/wiki/Robert_Louis_Stevenson "Robert Louis Stevenson"), also a son of the city, wrote that Edinburgh "is what Paris ought to be".[[33]](#cite_note-34)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Edinburgh](/wiki/History_of_Edinburgh "History of Edinburgh")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Surgeons%27_Hall%2C_Nicholson_Street_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3529543.jpg/250px-Surgeons%27_Hall%2C_Nicholson_Street_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3529543.jpg)](/wiki/File:Surgeons%27_Hall,_Nicholson_Street_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3529543.jpg)

[Surgeons' Hall](/wiki/Surgeons%27_Hall "Surgeons' Hall"), one of the [Greek Revival](/wiki/Greek_Revival_architecture "Greek Revival architecture") buildings that earned Edinburgh the nickname "Athens of the North"

The earliest known human habitation in the Edinburgh area was at [Cramond](/wiki/Cramond "Cramond"), where evidence was found of a [Mesolithic](/wiki/Mesolithic "Mesolithic") camp site dated to c. 8500 BC.[[34]](#cite_note-35) Traces of later [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") and [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") settlements have been found on Castle Rock, [Arthur's Seat](/wiki/Arthur%27s_Seat "Arthur's Seat"), [Craiglockhart Hill](/wiki/Craiglockhart_Hill "Craiglockhart Hill"), and the [Pentland Hills](/wiki/Pentland_Hills "Pentland Hills").[[35]](#cite_note-36)

When the [Romans](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") arrived in Lothian at the end of the 1st century AD, they found a [Brittonic](/wiki/Celtic_Britons "Celtic Britons") Celtic tribe whose name they recorded as the [Votadini](/wiki/Votadini "Votadini").[[36]](#cite_note-37) The Votadini transitioned into the [Gododdin](/wiki/Gododdin "Gododdin") kingdom in the [Early Middle Ages](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), with Eidyn serving as one of the kingdom's districts. During this period, the Castle Rock site, thought to have been the stronghold of Din Eidyn, emerged as the kingdom's major centre.[[37]](#cite_note-38) The medieval [Welsh-language](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") poem *[Y Gododdin](/wiki/Y_Gododdin "Y Gododdin")* describes a war band from across the Brittonic world who gathered in Eidyn before a fateful raid; this may describe a historical event around 600AD.[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41)

In 638 the Gododdin stronghold was besieged by forces loyal to [King Oswald](/wiki/Oswald_of_Northumbria "Oswald of Northumbria") of [Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria"), and around this time, control of Lothian passed to the [Angles](/wiki/Angles_(tribe) "Angles (tribe)"). Their influence continued for the next three centuries until around 950, when, during the reign of [Indulf](/wiki/Indulf "Indulf"), son of [Constantine II](/wiki/Constantine_II_of_Scotland "Constantine II of Scotland"), the "burh" (fortress), named in the 10th-century *[Pictish Chronicle](/wiki/Pictish_Chronicle "Pictish Chronicle")* as *oppidum Eden*,[[41]](#cite_note-42) was abandoned to the Scots. It thenceforth remained, for the most part, under their jurisdiction.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

The [royal burgh](/wiki/Royal_burgh "Royal burgh") was founded by [King David I](/wiki/David_I_of_Scotland "David I of Scotland") in the early 12th century on land belonging to the Crown, though the date of its charter is unknown.[[43]](#cite_note-44) The first documentary evidence of the medieval [burgh](/wiki/Burgh "Burgh") is a [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter"), c. 1124–1127, by King David I granting a [toft](/wiki/Burgage "Burgage") in *burgo meo de Edenesburg* to the [Priory of Dunfermline](/wiki/Dunfermline_Abbey "Dunfermline Abbey").[[44]](#cite_note-45) The [shire](/wiki/Shires_of_Scotland "Shires of Scotland") of Edinburgh seems also to have been created during David's reign, possibly covering all of Lothian at first, but by 1305 the eastern and western parts of Lothian had become [Haddingtonshire](/wiki/East_Lothian "East Lothian") and [Linlithgowshire](/wiki/West_Lothian "West Lothian"), leaving Edinburgh as the county town of a shire covering the central part of Lothian, which was called Edinburghshire or [Midlothian](/wiki/Midlothian "Midlothian") (the latter name being an informal, but commonly used, alternative until the county's name was legally changed in 1947).[[45]](#cite_note-Chalmers-46)[[46]](#cite_note-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Sunshine_on_the_Crags_%2828877122034%29.jpg/250px-Sunshine_on_the_Crags_%2828877122034%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sunshine_on_the_Crags_(28877122034).jpg)

Edinburgh, showing Arthur's Seat, one of the earliest known sites of human habitation in the area

Edinburgh was largely under English control from 1291 to 1314 and from 1333 to 1341, during the [Wars of Scottish Independence](/wiki/Wars_of_Scottish_Independence "Wars of Scottish Independence"). When the [English invaded Scotland in 1298](/wiki/English_invasion_of_Scotland_(1298) "English invasion of Scotland (1298)"), [Edward I of England](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England") chose not to enter Edinburgh but passed by it with his army.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

In the middle of the 14th century the French chronicler [Jean Froissart](/wiki/Jean_Froissart "Jean Froissart") described it as the capital of Scotland (c. 1365), and [James III](/wiki/James_III_of_Scotland "James III of Scotland") (1451–1488) referred to it in the 15th century as "the principal burgh of our kingdom".[[48]](#cite_note-49) In 1482 James III "granted and perpetually confirmed to the said Provost, Bailies, Clerk, Council, and Community, and their successors, the office of [Sheriff](/wiki/Sheriff_principal "Sheriff principal") within the Burgh for ever, to be exercised by the Provost for the time as Sheriff, and by the Bailies for the time as Sheriffsdepute conjunctly and severally; with full power to hold Courts, to punish transgressors not only by banishment but by death, to appoint officers of Court, and to do everything else appertaining to the office of Sheriff; as also to apply to their own proper use the fines and escheats arising out of the exercise of the said office."[[49]](#cite_note-50) Despite being [burnt by the English](/wiki/Burning_of_Edinburgh_(1544) "Burning of Edinburgh (1544)") in 1544, Edinburgh continued to develop and grow,[[50]](#cite_note-51) and was at the centre of events in the 16th-century [Scottish Reformation](/wiki/Scottish_Reformation "Scottish Reformation")[[51]](#cite_note-52) and 17th-century [Wars of the Covenant](/wiki/Covenanter "Covenanter").[[52]](#cite_note-53) In 1582 Edinburgh's town council was given a [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") by [King James VI and I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") permitting the establishment of a university;[[53]](#cite_note-54) founded as *Tounis College* (Town's College), the institution developed into the [University of Edinburgh](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh "University of Edinburgh"), which contributed to Edinburgh's central intellectual role in subsequent centuries.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

### 17th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 17th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Edinburgh_in_the_17thC_%28detail%29_by_Wenceslas_Hollar_%281670%29.jpg/250px-Edinburgh_in_the_17thC_%28detail%29_by_Wenceslas_Hollar_%281670%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_in_the_17thC_(detail)_by_Wenceslas_Hollar_(1670).jpg)

Edinburgh in the 17th century

In 1603 [King James VI of Scotland](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") succeeded to the English throne, uniting the crowns of Scotland and England, an event known as the [Union of the Crowns](/wiki/Union_of_the_Crowns "Union of the Crowns"), though the two kingdoms remained separate realms governed in [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union").[[55]](#cite_note-56) In 1638 [King Charles I](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England")'s attempt to introduce [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion") church forms in Scotland encountered stiff [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") opposition, culminating in the conflicts of the [Wars of the Three Kingdoms](/wiki/Wars_of_the_Three_Kingdoms "Wars of the Three Kingdoms").[[56]](#cite_note-57) Subsequent Scottish support for [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England")'s restoration to the throne of England resulted in Edinburgh's occupation by [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell")'s [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England") forces – the [New Model Army](/wiki/New_Model_Army "New Model Army") – in 1650.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

In the 17th century Edinburgh's boundaries were still defined by the city's defensive [town walls](/wiki/Edinburgh_town_walls "Edinburgh town walls"). As a result, the city's growing population was accommodated by increasing the height of the houses. Buildings of 11 storeys or more were common,[[58]](#cite_note-59) and have been described as forerunners of the modern-day skyscraper.[[59]](#cite_note-Council_on_Tall_Buildings_and_Urban_Habitat-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61) Most of these old structures were replaced by the predominantly [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") buildings seen in today's Old Town. In 1611 an act of parliament created the [High Constables of Edinburgh](/wiki/High_Constables_of_Edinburgh "High Constables of Edinburgh") to keep order in the city, thought to be the oldest statutory police force in the world.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

### 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 18th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/The_Parliament_Close_and_Public_Characters_Fifty_Years_Since.jpg/250px-The_Parliament_Close_and_Public_Characters_Fifty_Years_Since.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Parliament_Close_and_Public_Characters_Fifty_Years_Since.jpg)

A painting showing Edinburgh characters (based on [John Kay](/wiki/John_Kay_(caricaturist) "John Kay (caricaturist)")'s caricatures) behind St Giles' Cathedral in the late 18th century

Following the [Treaty of Union](/wiki/Treaty_of_Union "Treaty of Union") in 1706, the Parliaments of England and Scotland passed [Acts of Union](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") in 1706 and 1707 respectively, uniting the two kingdoms in the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") effective from 1 May 1707.[[62]](#cite_note-63) As a consequence, the [Parliament of Scotland](/wiki/Parliament_of_Scotland "Parliament of Scotland") merged with the [Parliament of England](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England") to form the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain"), which sat at [Westminster](/wiki/Palace_of_Westminster "Palace of Westminster") in London. The Union was opposed by many Scots, resulting in riots in the city.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

By the first half of the 18th century, Edinburgh was described as one of Europe's most densely populated, overcrowded, and unsanitary towns.[[64]](#cite_note-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66) Visitors were struck by the fact that the social classes shared the same urban space, even inhabiting the same [tenement](/wiki/Tenement "Tenement") buildings; although here a form of social segregation did prevail, whereby shopkeepers and tradesmen tended to occupy the cheaper-to-rent cellars and garrets, while the more well-to-do professional classes occupied the more expensive middle storeys.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

During the [Jacobite rising of 1745](/wiki/Jacobite_rising_of_1745 "Jacobite rising of 1745"), Edinburgh was briefly occupied by the Jacobite "Highland Army" before its march into England.[[67]](#cite_note-68) After its eventual defeat at [Culloden](/wiki/Battle_of_Culloden "Battle of Culloden"), there followed a period of reprisals and pacification, largely directed at the rebellious [clans](/wiki/Scottish_clan "Scottish clan").[[68]](#cite_note-69) In Edinburgh, the Town Council, keen to emulate London by initiating city improvements and expansion to the north of the castle,[[69]](#cite_note-70) reaffirmed its belief in the Union and loyalty to the [Hanoverian](/wiki/House_of_Hanover "House of Hanover") monarch [George III](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom") by its choice of names for the streets of the New Town: for example, [Rose](/wiki/Tudor_rose "Tudor rose") Street and [Thistle](/wiki/Thistle#Scottish_thistle "Thistle") Street; and for the royal family, [George Street](/wiki/George_Street,_Edinburgh "George Street, Edinburgh"), [Queen Street](/wiki/Queen_Street,_Edinburgh "Queen Street, Edinburgh"), Hanover Street, Frederick Street and [Princes Street](/wiki/Princes_Street "Princes Street") (in honour of George's two sons).[[70]](#cite_note-princeshist-71) The consistently geometric layout of the plan for the extension of Edinburgh was the result of a major competition in urban planning staged by the Town Council in 1766.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

In the second half of the century, the city was at the heart of the [Scottish Enlightenment](/wiki/Scottish_Enlightenment "Scottish Enlightenment"),[[72]](#cite_note-73) when thinkers like [David Hume](/wiki/David_Hume "David Hume"), [Adam Smith](/wiki/Adam_Smith "Adam Smith"), [James Hutton](/wiki/James_Hutton "James Hutton") and [Joseph Black](/wiki/Joseph_Black "Joseph Black") were familiar figures in its streets. Edinburgh became a major intellectual centre, earning it the nickname "Athens of the North" because of its many [neo-classical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") buildings and reputation for learning, recalling ancient Athens.[[73]](#cite_note-74) In the 18th-century novel *[The Expedition of Humphry Clinker](/wiki/The_Expedition_of_Humphry_Clinker "The Expedition of Humphry Clinker")* by [Tobias Smollett](/wiki/Tobias_Smollett "Tobias Smollett") one character describes Edinburgh as a "hotbed of genius".[[74]](#cite_note-75) Edinburgh was also a major centre for the Scottish book trade. The highly successful London bookseller [Andrew Millar](/wiki/Andrew_Millar_(publisher) "Andrew Millar (publisher)") was apprenticed there to James McEuen.[[75]](#cite_note-76)

From the 1770s onwards, the professional and business classes gradually deserted the Old Town in favour of the more elegant "one-family" residences of the New Town, a migration that changed the city's social character. According to the foremost historian of this development, "Unity of social feeling was one of the most valuable heritages of old Edinburgh, and its disappearance was widely and properly lamented."[[76]](#cite_note-77)

### 19th and 20th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 19th and 20th centuries")]

[![An aerial photo of Edinburgh with an aeroplane visible](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/79/Aerial_View_of_Edinburgh%2C_by_Alfred_Buckham%2C_from_about_1920.jpg/250px-Aerial_View_of_Edinburgh%2C_by_Alfred_Buckham%2C_from_about_1920.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aerial_View_of_Edinburgh,_by_Alfred_Buckham,_from_about_1920.jpg)

Edinburgh, c. 1920

Despite an enduring myth to the contrary,[[77]](#cite_note-78) Edinburgh became an industrial centre[[78]](#cite_note-79) with its traditional industries of printing, brewing and distilling continuing to grow in the 19th century and joined by new industries such as [rubber works](/wiki/Hunter_Boot_Ltd "Hunter Boot Ltd"), [engineering works](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") and others. By 1821 Edinburgh had been overtaken by [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow") as Scotland's largest city.[[79]](#cite_note-80) The city centre between Princes Street and George Street became a major commercial and shopping district, a development partly stimulated by the arrival of railways in the 1840s. The Old Town became an increasingly dilapidated, overcrowded slum with high mortality rates.[[80]](#cite_note-81) Improvements carried out under Lord Provost [William Chambers](/wiki/William_Chambers_(publisher) "William Chambers (publisher)") in the 1860s began the transformation of the area into the predominantly [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture") Old Town seen today.[[81]](#cite_note-82) More improvements followed in the early 20th century as a result of the work of [Patrick Geddes](/wiki/Patrick_Geddes "Patrick Geddes"),[[82]](#cite_note-83) but relative economic stagnation during the two world wars and beyond saw the Old Town deteriorate further before major [slum clearance](/wiki/Slum_clearance_in_the_United_Kingdom "Slum clearance in the United Kingdom") in the 1960s and 1970s began to reverse the process. New construction, such as of [Argyle House](/wiki/Argyle_House,_Edinburgh "Argyle House, Edinburgh") near the castle and the university building developments, which transformed the [George Square](/wiki/George_Square,_Edinburgh "George Square, Edinburgh") and Potterrow areas, proved highly controversial.[[83]](#cite_note-84)[[84]](#cite_note-h568-85)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Opening_of_the_Scottish_Parliament%2C_1999.jpg/250px-Opening_of_the_Scottish_Parliament%2C_1999.jpg)](/wiki/File:Opening_of_the_Scottish_Parliament,_1999.jpg)

Queen [Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") and [Donald Dewar](/wiki/Donald_Dewar "Donald Dewar") at the opening of the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament"), 1999

Since the 1990s a new "financial district", including the [Edinburgh International Conference Centre](/wiki/Edinburgh_International_Conference_Centre "Edinburgh International Conference Centre"), has grown mainly on demolished railway property to the west of the castle, stretching into [Fountainbridge](/wiki/Fountainbridge "Fountainbridge"), a run-down 19th-century industrial suburb which has undergone radical change since the 1980s with the demise of industrial and brewery premises. This ongoing development has enabled Edinburgh to maintain its place as the United Kingdom's second largest financial and administrative centre after London.[[85]](#cite_note-86)[[86]](#cite_note-87) Financial services now account for a third of all commercial office space in the city.[[87]](#cite_note-W_Rae,_Edinburgh_1994,_p.164-88) The development of [Edinburgh Park](/wiki/Edinburgh_Park "Edinburgh Park"), a new business and technology park covering 38 acres (15 ha), 4 mi (6 km) west of the city centre, has also contributed to the District Council's strategy for the city's major economic regeneration.[[87]](#cite_note-W_Rae,_Edinburgh_1994,_p.164-88)

In 1998 the [Scotland Act](/wiki/Scotland_Act_1998 "Scotland Act 1998"), which came into force the following year, established a [devolved](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") Scottish Parliament and Scottish Executive (renamed the Scottish Government since September 2007[[88]](#cite_note-89)). Both based in Edinburgh, they are responsible for governing Scotland while [reserved matters](/wiki/Reserved_and_excepted_matters "Reserved and excepted matters") such as defence, foreign affairs, and some elements of income tax remain the responsibility of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") in London.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 21st century")]

In 2022 Edinburgh was affected by the [2022 Scotland bin strikes](/wiki/2022_Scotland_bin_strikes "2022 Scotland bin strikes").[[90]](#cite_note-91) In 2023 Edinburgh became the first capital city in Europe to sign the global [Plant Based Treaty](/wiki/Plant-based_action_plan#Plant_Based_Treaty "Plant-based action plan"), which was introduced at [COP26](/wiki/2021_United_Nations_Climate_Change_Conference "2021 United Nations Climate Change Conference") in 2021 in Glasgow.[[91]](#cite_note-92) The [Scottish Greens](/wiki/Scottish_Greens "Scottish Greens") councillor Steve Burgess introduced the treaty. The Scottish Countryside Alliance and other farming groups called the treaty "anti-farming".[[92]](#cite_note-93)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geography")]

### Location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Location")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Edinburgh_Victora_Street_20211019.jpg/250px-Edinburgh_Victora_Street_20211019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_Victora_Street_20211019.jpg)

Victoria Street in October 2021

Situated in Scotland's [Central Belt](/wiki/Central_Belt "Central Belt"), Edinburgh lies on the southern shore of the Firth of Forth. The city centre is 2+1⁄2 mi (4.0 km) southwest of the shoreline of [Leith](/wiki/Leith "Leith") and 26 mi (42 km) inland, as the crow flies, from the east coast of Scotland and the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") at [Dunbar](/wiki/Dunbar "Dunbar").[[93]](#cite_note-94) While the early burgh grew up near the prominent Castle Rock, the modern city is often said to be built on [seven hills](/wiki/Hills_in_Edinburgh "Hills in Edinburgh"), namely [Calton Hill](/wiki/Calton_Hill "Calton Hill"), [Corstorphine Hill](/wiki/Corstorphine_Hill "Corstorphine Hill"), Craiglockhart Hill, [Braid Hill](/wiki/Braid_Hills "Braid Hills"), [Blackford Hill](/wiki/Blackford_Hill "Blackford Hill"), Arthur's Seat and the Castle Rock,[[94]](#cite_note-95) giving rise to allusions to the [seven hills of Rome](/wiki/Seven_hills_of_Rome "Seven hills of Rome").[[95]](#cite_note-96)

### Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Edinburgh._View_from_Calton_Hill.jpg/250px-Edinburgh._View_from_Calton_Hill.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh._View_from_Calton_Hill.jpg)

Edinburgh. View from Calton Hill.

Occupying a narrow gap between the Firth of Forth to the north and the [Pentland Hills](/wiki/Pentland_Hills "Pentland Hills") and their outrunners to the south, the city sprawls over a landscape which is the product of early volcanic activity and later periods of intensive glaciation.
[[96]](#cite_note-Edwards2005-97): 64–65 Igneous activity between 350 and 400 million years ago, coupled with [faulting](/wiki/Fault_(geology) "Fault (geology)"), led to the creation of tough [basalt](/wiki/Basalt "Basalt") [volcanic plugs](/wiki/Volcanic_plug "Volcanic plug"), which predominate over much of the area.[[96]](#cite_note-Edwards2005-97): 64–65 One such example is the Castle Rock which forced the advancing ice sheet to divide, sheltering the softer rock and forming a 1 mi-long (1.6 km) tail of material to the east, thus creating a distinctive [crag and tail](/wiki/Crag_and_tail "Crag and tail") formation.[[96]](#cite_note-Edwards2005-97): 64–65 Glacial erosion on the north side of the crag gouged a deep valley later filled by the now drained [Nor Loch](/wiki/Nor_Loch "Nor Loch"). These features, along with another hollow on the rock's south side, formed an ideal natural strongpoint upon which Edinburgh Castle was built.[[96]](#cite_note-Edwards2005-97): 64–65 Similarly, Arthur's Seat is the remains of a volcano dating from the [Carboniferous period](/wiki/Carboniferous_period "Carboniferous period"), which was eroded by a glacier moving west to east during the ice age.[[96]](#cite_note-Edwards2005-97): 64–65 Erosive action such as [plucking](/wiki/Plucking_(glaciation) "Plucking (glaciation)") and [abrasion](/wiki/Abrasion_(geology) "Abrasion (geology)") exposed the rocky crags to the west before leaving a tail of deposited glacial material swept to the east.[[97]](#cite_note-98) This process formed the distinctive [Salisbury Crags](/wiki/Salisbury_Crags "Salisbury Crags"), a series of [teschenite](/wiki/Theralite#Teschenites "Theralite") cliffs between Arthur's Seat and the location of the early burgh.[[98]](#cite_note-99) The residential areas of [Marchmont](/wiki/Marchmont "Marchmont") and [Bruntsfield](/wiki/Bruntsfield "Bruntsfield") are built along a series of [drumlin](/wiki/Drumlin "Drumlin") ridges south of the city centre, which were [deposited](/wiki/Glacial_deposition "Glacial deposition") as the glacier receded.[[96]](#cite_note-Edwards2005-97): 64–65

Other prominent landforms, such as [Calton Hill](/wiki/Calton_Hill "Calton Hill") and [Corstorphine Hill](/wiki/Corstorphine_Hill "Corstorphine Hill"), are also products of glacial erosion.[[96]](#cite_note-Edwards2005-97): 64–65 The Braid Hills and Blackford Hill are a series of small summits to the south of the city centre that command expansive views looking northwards over the urban area to the Firth of Forth.[[96]](#cite_note-Edwards2005-97): 64–65

[![Edinburgh aerial photograph panorama taken from Princes Street, city centre.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Edinburgh_cityscapes_-_aerial_-_panorama_-_2025-04-19_05.jpg/1280px-Edinburgh_cityscapes_-_aerial_-_panorama_-_2025-04-19_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_cityscapes_-_aerial_-_panorama_-_2025-04-19_05.jpg "Panorama of Edinburgh, 2025")

Panorama of Edinburgh, 2025

Edinburgh is drained by the river named the [Water of Leith](/wiki/Water_of_Leith "Water of Leith"), which rises at the Colzium Springs in the Pentland Hills and runs for 18 miles (29 km) through the south and west of the city, emptying into the Firth of Forth at Leith.[[99]](#cite_note-waterofleith-100) The nearest the river gets to the city centre is at [Dean Village](/wiki/Dean_Village "Dean Village") on the north-western edge of the New Town, where a deep gorge is spanned by [Thomas Telford](/wiki/Thomas_Telford "Thomas Telford")'s [Dean Bridge](/wiki/Dean_Bridge "Dean Bridge"), built in 1832 for the road to [Queensferry](/wiki/South_Queensferry "South Queensferry"). The [Water of Leith Walkway](/wiki/Water_of_Leith_Walkway "Water of Leith Walkway") is a mixed-use [trail](/wiki/Trail "Trail") that follows the course of the river for 19.6 km (12.2 mi) from Balerno to Leith.[[100]](#cite_note-walkway-101)

Excepting the shoreline of the Firth of Forth, Edinburgh is encircled by a [green belt](/wiki/Green_belt "Green belt"), designated in 1957, which stretches from [Dalmeny](/wiki/Dalmeny "Dalmeny") in the west to [Prestongrange](/wiki/Prestongrange "Prestongrange") in the east.[[101]](#cite_note-greenbelt-102) With an average width of 3.2 km (2 mi) the principal objectives of the green belt were to contain the outward expansion of the city and to prevent the agglomeration of urban areas.[[101]](#cite_note-greenbelt-102) Expansion affecting the green belt is strictly controlled but developments such as [Edinburgh Airport](/wiki/Edinburgh_Airport "Edinburgh Airport") and the [Royal Highland Showground](/wiki/Royal_Highland_Showground "Royal Highland Showground") at [Ingliston](/wiki/Ingliston "Ingliston") lie within the zone.[[101]](#cite_note-greenbelt-102) Similarly, suburbs such as [Juniper Green](/wiki/Juniper_Green "Juniper Green") and Balerno are situated on green belt land.[[101]](#cite_note-greenbelt-102) One feature of the Edinburgh green belt is the inclusion of parcels of land within the city which are designated green belt, even though they do not connect with the peripheral ring. Examples of these independent wedges of green belt include [Holyrood Park](/wiki/Holyrood_Park "Holyrood Park") and Corstorphine Hill.[[101]](#cite_note-greenbelt-102)

### Areas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Areas")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Edinburgh_map.png/250px-Edinburgh_map.png)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_map.png)

Map showing the areas of central Edinburgh

#### Early settlements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Early settlements")]

Edinburgh includes former towns and villages that retain much of their original character as settlements in existence before they were absorbed into the expanding city of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries.[[102]](#cite_note-103) Many areas, such as [Dalry](/wiki/Dalry,_Edinburgh "Dalry, Edinburgh"), contain residences that are multi-occupancy buildings known as [tenements](/wiki/Tenement "Tenement"), although the more southern and western parts of the city have traditionally been less built-up with a greater number of detached and semi-detached villas.[[103]](#cite_note-104)

The historic centre of Edinburgh is divided into two by the broad green swathe of [Princes Street Gardens](/wiki/Princes_Street_Gardens "Princes Street Gardens"). To the south, the view is dominated by Edinburgh Castle, built high on Castle Rock, and the long sweep of the Old Town descending towards Holyrood Palace. To the north lie Princes Street and the New Town.

The [West End](/wiki/West_End,_Edinburgh "West End, Edinburgh") includes the financial district, with insurance and banking offices as well as the Edinburgh International Conference Centre.

#### Old and New Towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Old and New Towns")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Edinburgh_Old_Town.jpg/250px-Edinburgh_Old_Town.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_Old_Town.jpg)

Skyline of the [Edinburgh Old Town](/wiki/Edinburgh_Old_Town "Edinburgh Old Town")

Edinburgh's Old and New Towns were listed as a UNESCO [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 1995 in recognition of the unique character of the Old Town with its medieval street layout and the planned Georgian New Town, including the adjoining Dean Village and Calton Hill areas. There are over 4,500 [listed buildings](/wiki/Listed_building_(United_Kingdom) "Listed building (United Kingdom)") within the city,[[10]](#cite_note-www.edinburgh.org_world-heritage-site-11)[[104]](#cite_note-105)[[105]](#cite_note-106) a higher proportion relative to area than any other city in the United Kingdom.

The castle is perched on top of a rocky crag (the remnant of an extinct volcano), and the [Royal Mile](/wiki/Royal_Mile "Royal Mile") runs down the crest of a ridge from it, terminating at Holyrood Palace. Minor streets (called closes or [wynds](/wiki/Wynd "Wynd")) lie on either side of the main spine, forming a herringbone pattern.[[106]](#cite_note-107) Due to space restrictions imposed by the narrowness of this landform, the Old Town became home to some of the earliest "high rise" residential buildings. Multi-storey dwellings known as *lands* were the norm from the 16th century onwards, with ten and eleven storeys being typical, and one even reaching fourteen or fifteen storeys.[[107]](#cite_note-108) Vaults below street level were inhabited to accommodate the influx of incomers, particularly [Irish immigrants](/wiki/Irish_migration_to_Great_Britain "Irish migration to Great Britain"), during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"). The street has several fine public buildings such as St Giles' Cathedral, the [City Chambers](/wiki/Edinburgh_City_Chambers "Edinburgh City Chambers") and the [Law Courts](/wiki/Parliament_House,_Edinburgh "Parliament House, Edinburgh"). Other places of historical interest nearby are [Greyfriars Kirkyard](/wiki/Greyfriars_Kirkyard "Greyfriars Kirkyard") and [Mary King's Close](/wiki/Mary_King%27s_Close "Mary King's Close"). The [Grassmarket](/wiki/Grassmarket "Grassmarket"), running deep below the castle, is connected by the steep double terraced Victoria Street. The street layout is typical of the old quarters of many Northern European cities.

The New Town was an 18th-century solution to the problem of an increasingly crowded city, which had been confined to the ridge sloping down from the castle. In 1766 a competition to design a "New Town" was won by [James Craig](/wiki/James_Craig_(architect) "James Craig (architect)"), a 27-year-old architect.[[108]](#cite_note-109) The plan was a rigid, ordered grid, which fitted in well with [Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment") ideas of rationality. The principal street was to be [George Street](/wiki/George_Street,_Edinburgh "George Street, Edinburgh"), running along the natural ridge to the north of what became known as the "Old Town". To either side of it are two other main streets: Princes Street and Queen Street. Princes Street has become Edinburgh's main shopping street and now has few of its [Georgian](/wiki/Georgian_architecture "Georgian architecture") buildings in their original state. The three main streets are connected by a series of streets running perpendicular to them. The east and west ends of George Street are terminated by [St Andrew Square](/wiki/St_Andrew_Square,_Edinburgh "St Andrew Square, Edinburgh") and [Charlotte Square](/wiki/Charlotte_Square "Charlotte Square") respectively.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/New_Town_of_Edinburgh_20140320-4.jpg/250px-New_Town_of_Edinburgh_20140320-4.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Town_of_Edinburgh_20140320-4.jpg)

Skyline of the [Edinburgh New Town](/wiki/Edinburgh_New_Town "Edinburgh New Town")

The latter, designed by [Robert Adam](/wiki/Robert_Adam "Robert Adam"), influenced the architectural style of the New Town into the early 19th century.[[109]](#cite_note-110) [Bute House](/wiki/Bute_House "Bute House"), the official residence of the [First Minister of Scotland](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland"), is on the north side of Charlotte Square.[[110]](#cite_note-111)

The hollow between the Old and New Towns was formerly the [Nor Loch](/wiki/Nor_Loch "Nor Loch"), which was created for the town's defence but came to be used by the inhabitants for dumping their [sewage](/wiki/Sewage "Sewage"). It was drained by the 1820s as part of the city's northward expansion. Craig's original plan included an ornamental canal on the site of the loch,[[70]](#cite_note-princeshist-71) but this idea was abandoned.[[111]](#cite_note-112) Soil excavated while laying the foundations of buildings in the New Town was dumped on the site of the [loch](/wiki/Loch "Loch") to create the slope connecting the Old and New Towns known as [The Mound](/wiki/The_Mound "The Mound").

In the middle of the 19th century the [National Gallery of Scotland](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Scotland "National Gallery of Scotland") and the [Royal Scottish Academy Building](/wiki/Royal_Scottish_Academy_Building "Royal Scottish Academy Building") were built on The Mound, and tunnels for the railway line between [Haymarket](/wiki/Haymarket_railway_station "Haymarket railway station") and [Waverley](/wiki/Edinburgh_Waverley_railway_station "Edinburgh Waverley railway station") stations were driven through it.

#### Southside

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Southside")]

Main article: [Southside, Edinburgh](/wiki/Southside,_Edinburgh "Southside, Edinburgh")

The Southside is a residential part of the city, which includes the districts of [St Leonards](/wiki/St_Leonard%27s,_Edinburgh "St Leonard's, Edinburgh"), [Marchmont](/wiki/Marchmont "Marchmont"), [Morningside](/wiki/Morningside,_Edinburgh "Morningside, Edinburgh"), [Newington](/wiki/Newington,_Edinburgh "Newington, Edinburgh"), [Sciennes](/wiki/Sciennes "Sciennes"), [the Grange](/wiki/The_Grange,_Edinburgh "The Grange, Edinburgh") and [Blackford](/wiki/Blackford,_Edinburgh "Blackford, Edinburgh"). The Southside is broadly analogous to the area covered formerly by the [Burgh Muir](/wiki/Burgh_Muir "Burgh Muir"), and was developed as a residential area after the opening of the [South Bridge](/wiki/South_Bridge,_Edinburgh "South Bridge, Edinburgh") in the 1780s. The Southside is particularly popular with families (many state and private schools are here), young professionals and students (the central University of Edinburgh campus is based around [George Square](/wiki/George_Square,_Edinburgh "George Square, Edinburgh") just north of Marchmont and [the Meadows](/wiki/The_Meadows_(park) "The Meadows (park)")), and [Napier University](/wiki/Napier_University "Napier University") (with major campuses around Merchiston and Morningside). The area is also well provided with hotels and "bed and breakfast" accommodation for visiting festival-goers. These districts often feature in works of fiction. For example, [Church Hill](/wiki/Church_Hill,_Edinburgh "Church Hill, Edinburgh") in Morningside, was the home of [Muriel Spark](/wiki/Muriel_Spark "Muriel Spark")'s Miss Jean Brodie,[[112]](#cite_note-113) and [Ian Rankin](/wiki/Ian_Rankin "Ian Rankin")'s [Inspector Rebus](/wiki/Inspector_Rebus "Inspector Rebus") lives in Marchmont and works in St Leonards.[[113]](#cite_note-114)

#### Leith

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Leith")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/The_Shore%2C_Leith.JPG/250px-The_Shore%2C_Leith.JPG)](/wiki/File:The_Shore,_Leith.JPG)

The Shore, Leith

[Leith](/wiki/Leith "Leith") was historically the port of Edinburgh, an arrangement of unknown date that was confirmed by the royal charter Robert the Bruce granted to the city in 1329.[[114]](#cite_note-115) The port developed a separate identity from Edinburgh, which to some extent it still retains, and it was a matter of great resentment when the two burghs merged in 1920 into the City of Edinburgh.[[115]](#cite_note-116) Even today, the parliamentary seat is known as "Edinburgh North and Leith". The loss of traditional industries and commerce (the [last shipyard](/wiki/Henry_Robb "Henry Robb") closed in 1983) resulted in economic decline.[[116]](#cite_note-117) The Edinburgh Waterfront development has transformed old dockland areas from Leith to Granton into residential areas with shopping and leisure facilities and helped rejuvenate the area. With the redevelopment, Edinburgh has gained the business of cruise liner companies, which now provide cruises to Norway, Sweden, Denmark, Germany, and the Netherlands.

The coastal suburb of [Portobello](/wiki/Portobello,_Edinburgh "Portobello, Edinburgh") is characterised by Georgian villas, Victorian tenements, a beach and [promenade](/wiki/Promenade "Promenade"), and cafés, bars, restaurants and independent shops. There are rowing and sailing clubs, a restored Victorian swimming pool, and [Victorian Turkish baths](/wiki/Victorian_Turkish_baths "Victorian Turkish baths").

#### Urban area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Urban area")]

The [urban area](/wiki/List_of_towns_and_cities_in_Scotland_by_population#Settlements "List of towns and cities in Scotland by population") of Edinburgh is almost entirely within the [City of Edinburgh Council](/wiki/The_City_of_Edinburgh_Council "The City of Edinburgh Council") boundary, merging with [Musselburgh](/wiki/Musselburgh "Musselburgh") in East Lothian. Towns within easy reach of the city boundary include [Inverkeithing](/wiki/Inverkeithing "Inverkeithing"), [Haddington](/wiki/Haddington,_East_Lothian "Haddington, East Lothian"), [Tranent](/wiki/Tranent "Tranent"), [Prestonpans](/wiki/Prestonpans "Prestonpans"), [Dalkeith](/wiki/Dalkeith "Dalkeith"), [Bonnyrigg](/wiki/Bonnyrigg "Bonnyrigg"), [Loanhead](/wiki/Loanhead "Loanhead"), [Penicuik](/wiki/Penicuik "Penicuik"), [Broxburn](/wiki/Broxburn,_West_Lothian "Broxburn, West Lothian"), [Livingston](/wiki/Livingston,_West_Lothian "Livingston, West Lothian") and [Dunfermline](/wiki/Dunfermline "Dunfermline"). Edinburgh lies at the heart of the Edinburgh & South East Scotland City region, with a population in 2014 of 1,339,380.[[117]](#cite_note-NRS_LA_Estimate-118)[[118]](#cite_note-City_Region_Definition-119)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Climate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Edinburgh_-_Princes_Street_Gardens_%2849944531007%29.jpg/250px-Edinburgh_-_Princes_Street_Gardens_%2849944531007%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_-_Princes_Street_Gardens_(49944531007).jpg)

Sunshine in [Princes Street Gardens](/wiki/Princes_Street_Gardens "Princes Street Gardens")

Like most of Scotland, Edinburgh has a cool [temperate](/wiki/Temperate_climate "Temperate climate") [maritime climate](/wiki/Maritime_climate "Maritime climate") (*Cfb*) which, despite its northerly latitude, is milder than places which lie at similar latitudes such as [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow") and [Labrador](/wiki/Labrador "Labrador").[[119]](#cite_note-temperature-120) The city's proximity to the sea mitigates any large variations in temperature or extremes of climate. Winter daytime temperatures rarely fall below freezing while summer temperatures are moderate, rarely exceeding 22 °C (72 °F).[[119]](#cite_note-temperature-120) The highest temperature recorded in the city was 31.6 °C (88.9 °F) on 25 July 2019[[119]](#cite_note-temperature-120) at Gogarbank, beating the previous record of 31 °C (88 °F) on 4 August 1975 at Edinburgh Airport.[[120]](#cite_note-121) The lowest temperature recorded in recent years was −14.6 °C (5.7 °F) during December 2010 at Gogarbank.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

Given Edinburgh's position between the coast and hills, it is renowned as "the windy city", with the prevailing wind direction coming from the south-west, which is often associated with warm, unstable air from the [North Atlantic Current](/wiki/North_Atlantic_Current "North Atlantic Current") that can give rise to rainfall – although considerably less than cities to the west, such as Glasgow.[[119]](#cite_note-temperature-120) Rainfall is distributed fairly evenly throughout the year.[[119]](#cite_note-temperature-120) Winds from an easterly direction are usually drier but considerably colder, and may be accompanied by [haar](/wiki/Haar_(fog) "Haar (fog)"), a persistent coastal fog. Vigorous Atlantic depressions, known as [European windstorms](/wiki/European_windstorm "European windstorm"), can affect the city between October and April.[[119]](#cite_note-temperature-120)

Located slightly north of the city centre, the weather station at the [Royal Botanic Garden Edinburgh](/wiki/Royal_Botanic_Garden_Edinburgh "Royal Botanic Garden Edinburgh") (RBGE) has been an official weather station for the [Met Office](/wiki/Met_Office "Met Office") since 1956.
The Met Office operates its own weather station at Gogarbank on the city's western outskirts, near [Edinburgh Airport](/wiki/Edinburgh_Airport "Edinburgh Airport").[[122]](#cite_note-123) This slightly inland station has a slightly wider temperature span between seasons, is cloudier and somewhat wetter, but differences are minor.

Temperature and rainfall records have been kept at the Royal Observatory since 1764.[[123]](#cite_note-124)

## Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Demography")]

Main article: [Demographics of Edinburgh](/wiki/Demographics_of_Edinburgh "Demographics of Edinburgh")

### Current

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Current")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Edinburgh_population_density_map%2C_2011_census.png/330px-Edinburgh_population_density_map%2C_2011_census.png)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_population_density_map,_2011_census.png)

Population density map

The most recent official population estimates (2020) are
506,520 for the locality (includes [Currie](/wiki/Currie,_Edinburgh "Currie, Edinburgh")),[[6]](#cite_note-NRS_Locality_Estimate-7) and
530,990 for the Edinburgh settlement (includes [Musselburgh](/wiki/Musselburgh "Musselburgh")).[[6]](#cite_note-NRS_Locality_Estimate-7)

Edinburgh has a high proportion of young adults, with 19.5% of the population in their 20s (exceeded only by Aberdeen) and 15.2% in their 30s which is the highest in Scotland. The proportion of Edinburgh's population born in the UK fell from 92% to 84% between 2001 and 2011, while the proportion of White Scottish-born fell from 78% to 70%. Of those Edinburgh residents born in the UK, 335,000 or 83% were born in Scotland, with 58,000 or 14% being born in England.[[128]](#cite_note-edinburgh.gov.uk-131)

Some 16,000 people or 3.2% of the city's population are of [Polish](/wiki/White_Polish "White Polish") descent. 77,800 people or 15.1% of Edinburgh's population class themselves as Non-White, which is an increase from 8.2% in 2011 and 4% in 2001. Of the Non-White population, the largest group by far is [Asian](/wiki/Asian-Scots "Asian-Scots"), totalling about 44 thousand people. Within the Asian population, people of [Chinese](/wiki/British_Chinese "British Chinese") descent are now the largest sub-group, with 15,076 people, amounting to about 2.9% of the city's total population. The city's population of [Indian](/wiki/British_Indian "British Indian") descent amounts to 12,414 (2.4% of the total population), while there are some 7,454 of [Pakistani](/wiki/British_Pakistanis "British Pakistanis") descent (1.5% of the total population). Although they account for only 2,685 people or 0.5% of the city's population, Edinburgh has the highest number and proportion of people of [Bangladeshi](/wiki/British_Bangladeshi "British Bangladeshi") descent in Scotland. Close to 12,000 people were born in African countries predominantly in [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria") and [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") (2.3% of the total population) and over 13,000 in the Americas. With the notable exception of Inner London, Edinburgh has a higher number of people born in the United States (over 6,500) than any other city in the UK.[[133]](#cite_note-2022census_Scot-136)

The proportion of people residing in Edinburgh born outside the UK was 23.5% in 2022, compared with 15.9% in 2011 and 8.3% in 2001. Below are the largest overseas-born groups in Edinburgh according to the 2022 census, alongside the two previous censuses.

### Historical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Historical")]

A census by the Edinburgh presbytery in 1592 recorded a population of 8,003 adults spread equally north and south of the High Street which runs along the spine of the ridge sloping down from the Castle.[[137]](#cite_note-143) In the 18th and 19th centuries, the population expanded rapidly, rising from 49,000 in 1751 to 136,000 in 1831, primarily due to migration from rural areas.[[96]](#cite_note-Edwards2005-97): 9 As the population grew, problems of overcrowding in the Old Town, particularly in the cramped [tenements](/wiki/Tenement "Tenement") that lined the present-day Royal Mile and the [Cowgate](/wiki/Cowgate "Cowgate"), were exacerbated.[[96]](#cite_note-Edwards2005-97): 9 Poor sanitary arrangements resulted in a high incidence of disease,[[96]](#cite_note-Edwards2005-97): 9 with outbreaks of [cholera](/wiki/Cholera "Cholera") occurring in 1832, 1848, and 1866.[[138]](#cite_note-144)

The construction of the New Town from 1767 onwards witnessed the migration of the professional and business classes from the difficult living conditions in the Old Town to the lower-density, higher quality surroundings taking shape on land to the north.
[[139]](#cite_note-etmoac46-145) Expansion southwards from the Old Town saw more tenements being built in the 19th century, giving rise to [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era") suburbs such as [Dalry](/wiki/Dalry,_Edinburgh "Dalry, Edinburgh"), Newington, Marchmont and Bruntsfield.[[139]](#cite_note-etmoac46-145)

Early 20th-century population growth coincided with lower-density suburban development. As the city expanded to the south and west, detached and semi-detached villas with large gardens replaced tenements as the predominant building style. Nonetheless, the 2001 census revealed that over 55% of Edinburgh's population were still living in tenements or blocks of flats, a figure in line with other Scottish cities, but much higher than other British cities, and even central London.[[140]](#cite_note-dwellings-146)

From the early to mid 20th century, the growth in population, together with slum clearance in the Old Town and other areas, such as [Dumbiedykes](/wiki/Dumbiedykes "Dumbiedykes"), [Leith](/wiki/Leith "Leith"), and [Fountainbridge](/wiki/Fountainbridge "Fountainbridge"), led to the creation of new estates such as [Stenhouse](/wiki/Stenhouse,_Edinburgh "Stenhouse, Edinburgh") and [Saughton](/wiki/Saughton "Saughton"), [Craigmillar](/wiki/Craigmillar "Craigmillar") and [Niddrie](/wiki/Niddrie,_Edinburgh "Niddrie, Edinburgh"), [Pilton](/wiki/Pilton,_Edinburgh "Pilton, Edinburgh") and [Muirhouse](/wiki/Muirhouse "Muirhouse"), [Piershill](/wiki/Piershill "Piershill"), and [Sighthill](/wiki/Sighthill,_Edinburgh "Sighthill, Edinburgh").[[141]](#cite_note-147)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/St._Giles%2C_Edinburgh.jpg/250px-St._Giles%2C_Edinburgh.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Giles,_Edinburgh.jpg)

The High Kirk of Edinburgh, also known as [St Giles' Cathedral](/wiki/St_Giles%27_Cathedral "St Giles' Cathedral")

As per the 2022 census, 13% of the population belonged to the Church of Scotland and 10% to the Catholic Church.[[142]](#cite_note-148)

[Saint Giles](/wiki/Saint_Giles "Saint Giles") is historically the patron saint of Edinburgh.[[143]](#cite_note-149) [St Cuthbert's](/wiki/St_Cuthbert%27s_Church,_Edinburgh "St Cuthbert's Church, Edinburgh"), situated at the west end of Princes Street Gardens in the shadow of Edinburgh Castle and St Giles' can lay claim to being the oldest Christian sites in the city,[[144]](#cite_note-150) though the present St Cuthbert's, designed by [Hippolyte Blanc](/wiki/Hippolyte_Blanc "Hippolyte Blanc"), was dedicated in 1894.[[145]](#cite_note-151)

Other Church of Scotland churches include [Greyfriars Kirk](/wiki/Greyfriars_Kirk "Greyfriars Kirk"), the [Canongate Kirk](/wiki/Canongate_Kirk "Canongate Kirk"), [The New Town Church](/wiki/Edinburgh:_New_Town_Church "Edinburgh: New Town Church") and the [Barclay Church](/wiki/Edinburgh_Barclay_Church "Edinburgh Barclay Church"). The [Church of Scotland Offices](/wiki/Church_of_Scotland_Offices "Church of Scotland Offices") are in Edinburgh,[[146]](#cite_note-152) as is the [Assembly Hall](/wiki/General_Assembly_Hall_of_the_Church_of_Scotland "General Assembly Hall of the Church of Scotland") where the annual [General Assembly](/wiki/General_Assembly_of_the_Church_of_Scotland "General Assembly of the Church of Scotland") is held.[[147]](#cite_note-153)

The [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of St Andrews and Edinburgh](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_St_Andrews_and_Edinburgh "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of St Andrews and Edinburgh") has 27 parishes across the city.[[148]](#cite_note-154) The [archbishop of St Andrews and Edinburgh](/wiki/Archbishop_of_St_Andrews_and_Edinburgh "Archbishop of St Andrews and Edinburgh") has his official residence in [Greenhill](/wiki/Greenhill,_Edinburgh "Greenhill, Edinburgh"),[[149]](#cite_note-155) the diocesan offices are in nearby [Marchmont](/wiki/Marchmont "Marchmont"),[[150]](#cite_note-156) and its cathedral is [St Mary's Cathedral, Edinburgh](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Cathedral,_Edinburgh_(Catholic) "St Mary's Cathedral, Edinburgh (Catholic)"). The [Diocese of Edinburgh](/wiki/Diocese_of_Edinburgh "Diocese of Edinburgh") of the [Scottish Episcopal Church](/wiki/Scottish_Episcopal_Church "Scottish Episcopal Church") has over 50 churches, half of them in the city.[[151]](#cite_note-157) Its centre is the late 19th-century [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture") style [St Mary's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Cathedral,_Edinburgh_(Episcopal) "St Mary's Cathedral, Edinburgh (Episcopal)") in the West End's Palmerston Place.[[152]](#cite_note-158) Orthodox Christianity is represented by Pan, [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_Orthodox_Church "Romanian Orthodox Church") and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") Orthodox churches, including [St Andrew's Orthodox Church](/wiki/St_Andrew%27s_Orthodox_Church,_Edinburgh "St Andrew's Orthodox Church, Edinburgh"), part of the [Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of Thyateira and Great Britain](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Archdiocese_of_Thyateira_and_Great_Britain "Greek Orthodox Archdiocese of Thyateira and Great Britain").[[153]](#cite_note-159) There are several independent churches in the city, both [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") and [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), including [Charlotte Chapel](/wiki/Charlotte_Chapel_(Edinburgh) "Charlotte Chapel (Edinburgh)"), [Carrubbers Christian Centre](/wiki/Carrubbers_Christian_Centre "Carrubbers Christian Centre"), [Bellevue Chapel](/wiki/Bellevue_Chapel "Bellevue Chapel") and [Sacred Heart](/wiki/Sacred_Heart,_Edinburgh "Sacred Heart, Edinburgh").[[154]](#cite_note-160) There are also churches belonging to [Quakers](/wiki/Quaker_Meeting_House,_Edinburgh "Quaker Meeting House, Edinburgh"), [Christadelphians](/wiki/Christadelphians "Christadelphians"),[[155]](#cite_note-161) [Seventh-day Adventists](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church"), [Church of Christ, Scientist](/wiki/Church_of_Christ,_Scientist "Church of Christ, Scientist"), [The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") (LDS Church) and [Elim Pentecostal Church](/wiki/Elim_Pentecostal_Church "Elim Pentecostal Church").
