Muslims have several places of worship across the city. [Edinburgh Central Mosque](/wiki/Edinburgh_Central_Mosque "Edinburgh Central Mosque"), the largest Islamic place of worship, is located in Potterrow on the city's Southside, near Bristo Square. Construction was largely financed by a gift from King [Fahd of Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Fahd_of_Saudi_Arabia "Fahd of Saudi Arabia")[[157]](#cite_note-163) and was completed in 1998.[[158]](#cite_note-164) There is also an [Ahmadiyya](/wiki/Ahmadiyya "Ahmadiyya") Muslim community.[[159]](#cite_note-165) The first recorded presence of a [Jewish community](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Scotland#17th–19th_centuries "History of the Jews in Scotland") in Edinburgh dates back to the late 18th century.[[160]](#cite_note-166) Edinburgh's [Orthodox](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") synagogue, opened in 1932, is in Salisbury Road and can accommodate a congregation of 2000. A [Liberal Jewish congregation](/wiki/Liberal_Judaism_(United_Kingdom) "Liberal Judaism (United Kingdom)") also meets in the city. A [Sikh](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") [gurdwara](/wiki/Gurdwara "Gurdwara") and a [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") [mandir](/wiki/Mandir "Mandir") are located in Leith.[[161]](#cite_note-167)[[162]](#cite_note-168) The city also has a [Brahma Kumaris](/wiki/Brahma_Kumaris "Brahma Kumaris") centre in the Polwarth area.[[163]](#cite_note-169)

The Edinburgh Buddhist Centre, run by the [Triratna Buddhist Community](/wiki/Triratna_Buddhist_Community "Triratna Buddhist Community"), formerly situated in Melville Terrace, now runs sessions at the Healthy Life Centre, Bread Street.[[164]](#cite_note-170) Other Buddhist traditions are represented by groups which meet in the capital: the Community of Interbeing (followers of [Thich Nhat Hanh](/wiki/Thich_Nhat_Hanh "Thich Nhat Hanh")), [Rigpa](/wiki/Rigpa "Rigpa"), Samye Dzong, [Theravadin](/wiki/Theravadin "Theravadin"), [Pure Land](/wiki/Pure_Land_Buddhism "Pure Land Buddhism") and [Shambala](/wiki/Shambhala_Buddhism "Shambhala Buddhism"). There is a [Sōtō Zen](/wiki/S%C5%8Dt%C5%8D_Zen "Sōtō Zen") Priory in Portobello[[165]](#cite_note-171) and a Theravadin Thai Buddhist Monastery in Slateford Road.[[166]](#cite_note-172) Edinburgh is home to a [Baháʼí](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith") community,[[167]](#cite_note-173) and a [Theosophical Society](/wiki/Theosophical_Society "Theosophical Society") meets in Great King Street.[[168]](#cite_note-174) Edinburgh has an Inter-Faith Association.[[169]](#cite_note-175)

Edinburgh has over 39 [graveyards and cemeteries](/wiki/List_of_graveyards_and_cemeteries_in_Edinburgh "List of graveyards and cemeteries in Edinburgh"), many of which are listed and of historical character, including several former church burial grounds.[[170]](#cite_note-Golledge-176) Examples include [Old Calton Burial Ground](/wiki/Old_Calton_Burial_Ground "Old Calton Burial Ground"), [Greyfriars Kirkyard](/wiki/Greyfriars_Kirkyard "Greyfriars Kirkyard") and [Dean Cemetery](/wiki/Dean_Cemetery "Dean Cemetery").[[171]](#cite_note-OldCarltonBurial-177)[[172]](#cite_note-Greyfriars-178)[[173]](#cite_note-DeanCemetery-179)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Edinburgh](/wiki/Economy_of_Edinburgh "Economy of Edinburgh")

Further information: [Economy of Scotland](/wiki/Economy_of_Scotland "Economy of Scotland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Museum_on_the_mound%2C_Edingburgh.jpg/250px-Museum_on_the_mound%2C_Edingburgh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_on_the_mound,_Edingburgh.jpg)

The [Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Bank_of_Scotland "Bank of Scotland")'s head office in central Edinburgh

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/20231022_Rockstar_North.jpg/250px-20231022_Rockstar_North.jpg)](/wiki/File:20231022_Rockstar_North.jpg)

Headquarters of [Rockstar North](/wiki/Rockstar_North "Rockstar North") situated in Edinburgh

Edinburgh has the second-strongest[*[vague](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness")*] economy of any city in the United Kingdom behind [London](/wiki/London "London") and the highest percentage of professionals in the UK with 43% of the population holding a degree-level or professional qualification.[[174]](#cite_note-Work_In_Edinburgh_-_Jobs,_Employment,_Careers_And_Recruitment_Information-180) According to the Centre for International Competitiveness, it is the second most competitive major city in the United Kingdom.[[175]](#cite_note-181) In 2023, its [gross domestic product per capita](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product_per_capita "Gross domestic product per capita") of £69,809 surpassed London's for the first time.[[176]](#cite_note-182)[[177]](#cite_note-183) It also had the highest [gross value added](/wiki/Gross_value_added "Gross value added") per employee of any city in the UK outside London, measuring £57,594 in 2010.[[178]](#cite_note-184) It was named European Best Large City of the Future for Foreign Direct Investment and Best Large City for Foreign Direct Investment Strategy in the *[Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times")* magazine in 2012.[[179]](#cite_note-185)

As the centre of [Scotland's government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") and [legal system](/wiki/Scots_law "Scots law"), the public sector plays a central role in Edinburgh's economy. Many departments of the Scottish Government are in the city, including the headquarters of the government at [St Andrew's House](/wiki/St_Andrew%27s_House "St Andrew's House"), the official residence of the [First Minister](/wiki/First_Minister_of_Scotland "First Minister of Scotland") at [Bute House](/wiki/Bute_House "Bute House") and Scottish Government offices at [Victoria Quay](/wiki/Victoria_Quay,_Edinburgh "Victoria Quay, Edinburgh"). Other major sectors across the city include administrative and support services, the education sector, public administration and defence, the health and social care sector, scientific and technical services, and construction and manufacturing.[[180]](#cite_note-186) When the £1.3bn Edinburgh & South East Scotland City Region Deal[[181]](#cite_note-187) was signed in 2018, the region's Gross Value Added (GVA) contribution to the Scottish economy was cited as £33bn, or 33% of the country's output. The City Region Deal funds a range of "Data Driven Innovation" hubs which are using data to innovate in the region, recognising the region's strengths in technology and data science, the growing importance of the data economy, and the need to tackle the digital skills gap, as a route to social and economic prosperity.[[182]](#cite_note-188)[[183]](#cite_note-189)[[184]](#cite_note-190)

Tourism is also an important element in the city's economy. As a World Heritage Site, tourists visit historical sites such as Edinburgh Castle, the Palace of Holyroodhouse, and the Old and New Towns. Their numbers are augmented in August each year during the [Edinburgh Festivals](/wiki/Edinburgh_Festival "Edinburgh Festival"), which attract over 4 million visitors, and generate over £400M for the local economy.[[185]](#cite_note-:2-191) In May 2024, unemployment in Edinburgh was at 3.5%, in line with the Scottish average of 3.7%.[[186]](#cite_note-192)[[187]](#cite_note-193) In 2022 Edinburgh was the second most visited city in the United Kingdom, behind London, by overseas visitors.[[188]](#cite_note-194)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Festivals and celebrations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Festivals and celebrations")]

#### Edinburgh festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Edinburgh festivals")]

Main article: [List of Edinburgh festivals](/wiki/List_of_Edinburgh_festivals "List of Edinburgh festivals")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Edinburgh_Tattoo_2010_%284946272332%29.jpg/250px-Edinburgh_Tattoo_2010_%284946272332%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_Tattoo_2010_(4946272332).jpg)

[Edinburgh Castle](/wiki/Edinburgh_Castle "Edinburgh Castle") illuminated during the [Edinburgh Military Tattoo](/wiki/Edinburgh_Military_Tattoo "Edinburgh Military Tattoo")

The city hosts a series of festivals that run between the end of July and early September each year. The best known of these events are the [Edinburgh Festival Fringe](/wiki/Edinburgh_Fringe "Edinburgh Fringe"), the [Edinburgh International Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_International_Festival "Edinburgh International Festival"), the [Edinburgh Military Tattoo](/wiki/Edinburgh_Military_Tattoo "Edinburgh Military Tattoo"), the [Edinburgh Art Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_Art_Festival "Edinburgh Art Festival"), the [Edinburgh International Book Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_International_Book_Festival "Edinburgh International Book Festival") and [Edinburgh International Film Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_International_Film_Festival "Edinburgh International Film Festival").[[189]](#cite_note-195)

The longest established of these festivals is the Edinburgh International Festival (EIF) and Edinburgh International Film Festival (EIFF). Both festivals were first held in 1947.[[190]](#cite_note-196)[[191]](#cite_note-197) EIF consists mainly of a programme of high-profile theatre productions and classical music performances, featuring international directors, conductors, theatre companies and orchestras.[[192]](#cite_note-198) Edinburgh International Film Festival is the world's oldest continually running [film festival](/wiki/Film_festival "Film festival"). EIFF is globally renowned for world, international and UK premieres, high-profile film talent, significant filmmaking competitions and prominent industry guests.[[193]](#cite_note-199)

The Edinburgh Fringe, which began as a programme of marginal acts alongside the "official" Festival, has become the world's largest performing arts festival. In 2023, over 3700 different shows were staged in 300 venues across the city.[[185]](#cite_note-:2-191)[[194]](#cite_note-200) Comedy has become one of the mainstays of the Fringe, with many comedians getting their first 'break' there, often by being chosen to receive the [Edinburgh Comedy Award](/wiki/Edinburgh_Comedy_Awards "Edinburgh Comedy Awards").[[195]](#cite_note-201) The Edinburgh Military Tattoo occupies the Castle Esplanade every night for three weeks each August, with massed [pipe bands](/wiki/Pipe_band "Pipe band") and [military bands](/wiki/Military_band "Military band") drawn from around the world. Performances end with a short fireworks display.

As well as the summer festivals, [many other festivals are held during the rest of the year](/wiki/List_of_Edinburgh_festivals "List of Edinburgh festivals"), including [Edinburgh International Science Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_International_Science_Festival "Edinburgh International Science Festival").[[196]](#cite_note-202) The summer of 2020 was the first time in its 70-year history that the Edinburgh festival was not run, being cancelled due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[197]](#cite_note-203) This affected many of the tourist-focused businesses in Edinburgh which depend on the various festivals over summer to return an annual profit.[[198]](#cite_note-204)

#### Edinburgh's Hogmanay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Edinburgh's Hogmanay")]

Main article: [Edinburgh's Hogmanay](/wiki/Edinburgh%27s_Hogmanay "Edinburgh's Hogmanay")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Edinburgh_Hogmanay_Longship.jpg/250px-Edinburgh_Hogmanay_Longship.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_Hogmanay_Longship.jpg)

A Viking [longship](/wiki/Longship "Longship") being burnt during Edinburgh's annual [Hogmanay](/wiki/Hogmanay "Hogmanay") celebrations

The annual Edinburgh [Hogmanay](/wiki/Hogmanay "Hogmanay") celebration was originally an informal street party focused on the [Tron Kirk](/wiki/Tron_Kirk "Tron Kirk") in the Old Town's High Street. Since 1993, it has been officially organised with the focus moved to Princes Street. In 1996, over 300,000 people attended, leading to ticketing of the main street party in later years up to a limit of 100,000 tickets.[[199]](#cite_note-Hogmanay-205) Hogmanay now covers four days of processions, concerts and fireworks, with the street party beginning on Hogmanay. Alternative tickets are available for entrance into the Princes Street Gardens concert and [Cèilidh](/wiki/C%C3%A8ilidh "Cèilidh"), where well-known artists perform and ticket holders can participate in traditional Scottish cèilidh dancing. The event attracts thousands of people from all over the world.[[199]](#cite_note-Hogmanay-205)

#### Beltane and other festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Beltane and other festivals")]

On the night of 30 April the [Beltane Fire Festival](/wiki/Beltane_Fire_Festival "Beltane Fire Festival") takes place on Calton Hill, involving a procession followed by scenes inspired by [pagan](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism") old spring fertility celebrations.[[200]](#cite_note-206) At the beginning of October each year the [Dussehra](/wiki/Navratri "Navratri") Hindu Festival is also held on Calton Hill.[[201]](#cite_note-207)

### Music, theatre and film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Music, theatre and film")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Usher_Hall_from_Cambridge_Street_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2355109.jpg/250px-Usher_Hall_from_Cambridge_Street_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2355109.jpg)](/wiki/File:Usher_Hall_from_Cambridge_Street_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2355109.jpg)

The [Usher Hall](/wiki/Usher_Hall "Usher Hall")

Outside the festival season, Edinburgh supports several theatres and production companies. The [Royal Lyceum Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Lyceum_Theatre "Royal Lyceum Theatre") has its own company, while the [King's Theatre](/wiki/King%27s_Theatre,_Edinburgh "King's Theatre, Edinburgh"), [Edinburgh Festival Theatre](/wiki/Edinburgh_Festival_Theatre "Edinburgh Festival Theatre") and [Edinburgh Playhouse](/wiki/Edinburgh_Playhouse "Edinburgh Playhouse") stage large touring shows. The [Traverse Theatre](/wiki/Traverse_Theatre "Traverse Theatre") presents a more contemporary repertoire. [Amateur theatre companies](/wiki/Edinburgh_amateur_theatre "Edinburgh amateur theatre") productions are staged at the [Bedlam Theatre](/wiki/Bedlam_Theatre "Bedlam Theatre"), [Church Hill Theatre](/wiki/Church_Hill_Theatre "Church Hill Theatre") and [King's Theatre](/wiki/King%27s_Theatre,_Edinburgh "King's Theatre, Edinburgh") among others.[[202]](#cite_note-208) The [Usher Hall](/wiki/Usher_Hall "Usher Hall") is Edinburgh's premier venue for classical music, as well as occasional popular music concerts.[[203]](#cite_note-209) It was the venue for the [Eurovision Song Contest 1972](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_1972 "Eurovision Song Contest 1972"). Other halls staging music and theatre include [The Hub](/wiki/The_Hub_(Edinburgh) "The Hub (Edinburgh)"), the [Assembly Rooms](/wiki/Assembly_Rooms_(Edinburgh) "Assembly Rooms (Edinburgh)") and the [Queen's Hall](/wiki/Queen%27s_Hall,_Edinburgh "Queen's Hall, Edinburgh"). The [Scottish Chamber Orchestra](/wiki/Scottish_Chamber_Orchestra "Scottish Chamber Orchestra") is based in Edinburgh.[[204]](#cite_note-210)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Traverse_Theatre%2C_Edinburgh.JPG/250px-Traverse_Theatre%2C_Edinburgh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Traverse_Theatre,_Edinburgh.JPG)

[Traverse Theatre](/wiki/Traverse_Theatre "Traverse Theatre")

Edinburgh has two repertory cinemas, [The Cameo](/wiki/The_Cameo,_Edinburgh "The Cameo, Edinburgh") and the [Edinburgh Filmhouse](/wiki/Edinburgh_Filmhouse "Edinburgh Filmhouse"), as well as the independent [Dominion Cinema](/wiki/Dominion_Cinema "Dominion Cinema") and a range of [multiplexes](/wiki/Multiplex_(cinema) "Multiplex (cinema)").[[205]](#cite_note-211) Large concerts are occasionally staged at [Murrayfield Stadium](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium") and [Meadowbank Stadium](/wiki/Meadowbank_Stadium "Meadowbank Stadium"), while mid-sized events take place at smaller venues such as [O2 Academy Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_Corn_Exchange "Edinburgh Corn Exchange"). In 2010, [PRS for Music](/wiki/PRS_for_Music "PRS for Music") listed Edinburgh among the UK's top ten 'most musical' cities.[[206]](#cite_note-212) Several city pubs are well known for their live performances of [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music").[[207]](#cite_note-213)

Nightclub venues within the city host [electronic dance music](/wiki/Electronic_dance_music "Electronic dance music") events.[[208]](#cite_note-214)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Media")]

The main local newspaper is the *[Edinburgh Evening News](/wiki/Edinburgh_Evening_News "Edinburgh Evening News")*. It is owned and published alongside its sister titles *[The Scotsman](/wiki/The_Scotsman "The Scotsman")* and *[Scotland on Sunday](/wiki/Scotland_on_Sunday "Scotland on Sunday")* by [JPIMedia](/wiki/JPIMedia "JPIMedia").[[209]](#cite_note-215) Student newspapers include, *[The Journal](/wiki/The_Journal_(student_newspaper) "The Journal (student newspaper)")* Scotland wide Universities, and *[The Student](/wiki/The_Student_(newspaper) "The Student (newspaper)")* [University of Edinburgh](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh "University of Edinburgh") which was founded in 1887. Community newspapers include *The Spurtle* from Broughton, *Spokes Bulletin*, and *The Edinburgh Reporter*.

The city has many commercial radio stations including [Forth 1](/wiki/Forth_1 "Forth 1"), a station which broadcasts mainstream chart music, Greatest Hits Edinburgh on DAB which plays classic hits and Edge Radio.[[210]](#cite_note-216) [Capital Scotland](/wiki/Capital_Scotland "Capital Scotland") and [Heart Scotland](/wiki/Heart_Scotland "Heart Scotland") also have transmitters covering Edinburgh. Along with the UK national radio stations, [BBC Radio Scotland](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Scotland "BBC Radio Scotland") and the Gaelic language service [BBC Radio nan Gàidheal](/wiki/BBC_Radio_nan_G%C3%A0idheal "BBC Radio nan Gàidheal") are also broadcast. DAB digital radio is broadcast over two local multiplexes. [BFBS](/wiki/BFBS "BFBS") Radio broadcasts from studios on the base at Dreghorn Barracks across the city on 98.5FM as part of its UK Bases network. Small-scale DAB started in October 2022 with community stations on board.

Television, along with most radio services, is broadcast to the city from the [Craigkelly transmitting station](/wiki/Craigkelly_transmitting_station "Craigkelly transmitting station") situated in Fife on the opposite side of the Firth of Forth[[211]](#cite_note-217) and the [Black Hill transmitting station](/wiki/Black_Hill_transmitting_station "Black Hill transmitting station") in [North Lanarkshire](/wiki/North_Lanarkshire "North Lanarkshire") to the west. There are no television stations based in the city. Edinburgh Television existed in the late 1990s to early 2003[[212]](#cite_note-218) and [STV Edinburgh](/wiki/STV_Edinburgh "STV Edinburgh") existed from 2015 to 2018.[[213]](#cite_note-219)[[214]](#cite_note-220)

### Museums, libraries, and galleries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Museums, libraries, and galleries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/National_Gallery_of_Scotland_2005.jpg/250px-National_Gallery_of_Scotland_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Gallery_of_Scotland_2005.jpg)

[National Gallery of Scotland](/wiki/Scottish_National_Gallery "Scottish National Gallery")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Museum_of_Scotland.jpg/250px-Museum_of_Scotland.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_of_Scotland.jpg)

[National Museum of Scotland](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Scotland "National Museum of Scotland")

Edinburgh has many museums and libraries. These include the [National Museum of Scotland](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Scotland "National Museum of Scotland"), the [National Library of Scotland](/wiki/National_Library_of_Scotland "National Library of Scotland"), [National War Museum](/wiki/National_War_Museum "National War Museum"), the [Museum of Edinburgh](/wiki/Museum_of_Edinburgh "Museum of Edinburgh"), [Surgeons' Hall Museum](/wiki/Surgeons%27_Hall "Surgeons' Hall"), the [Writers' Museum](/wiki/Writers%27_Museum "Writers' Museum"), the [Museum of Childhood](/wiki/Museum_of_Childhood_(Edinburgh) "Museum of Childhood (Edinburgh)") and [Dynamic Earth](/wiki/Dynamic_Earth_(Edinburgh) "Dynamic Earth (Edinburgh)"). The [Museum on The Mound](/wiki/Museum_on_the_Mound "Museum on the Mound") has exhibits on money and banking.[[215]](#cite_note-221) [Edinburgh Zoo](/wiki/Edinburgh_Zoo "Edinburgh Zoo"), covering 82 acres (33 ha) on Corstorphine Hill, is the second most visited paid tourist attraction in Scotland,[[216]](#cite_note-begin-222) and was previously home to two [giant pandas](/wiki/Giant_panda "Giant panda"), Tian Tian and Yang Guang, on loan from the People's Republic of China. Edinburgh is also home to [The Royal Yacht Britannia](/wiki/HMY_Britannia "HMY Britannia"), decommissioned in 1997 and now a five-star visitor attraction and evening events venue permanently berthed at [Ocean Terminal](/wiki/Ocean_Terminal,_Edinburgh "Ocean Terminal, Edinburgh").

Edinburgh contains Scotland's three [National Galleries](/wiki/National_Galleries_Scotland "National Galleries Scotland") of Art as well as smaller art galleries.[[217]](#cite_note-223) The national collection is housed in the [Scottish National Gallery](/wiki/Scottish_National_Gallery "Scottish National Gallery"), located on The Mound, comprising the linked National Gallery of Scotland building and the [Royal Scottish Academy building](/wiki/Royal_Scottish_Academy_building "Royal Scottish Academy building"). Contemporary collections are shown in the [Scottish National Gallery of Modern Art](/wiki/Scottish_National_Gallery_of_Modern_Art "Scottish National Gallery of Modern Art"), which occupies a split site at Belford. The [Scottish National Portrait Gallery](/wiki/Scottish_National_Portrait_Gallery "Scottish National Portrait Gallery") on Queen Street focuses on portraits and photography. The council-owned [City Art Centre](/wiki/City_Art_Centre "City Art Centre") in Market Street mounts regular art exhibitions. Across the road, [The Fruitmarket Gallery](/wiki/Fruitmarket_Gallery "Fruitmarket Gallery") offers world-class exhibitions of contemporary art, featuring work by British and international artists with both emerging and established international reputations.[[218]](#cite_note-224)

The city hosts several of Scotland's galleries and organisations dedicated to contemporary visual art. Significant strands of this infrastructure include [Creative Scotland](/wiki/Creative_Scotland "Creative Scotland"), [Edinburgh College of Art](/wiki/Edinburgh_College_of_Art "Edinburgh College of Art"), [Talbot Rice Gallery](/wiki/Talbot_Rice_Gallery "Talbot Rice Gallery") (University of Edinburgh), [Collective Gallery](/wiki/Collective_Gallery "Collective Gallery") (based at the [City Observatory](/wiki/City_Observatory "City Observatory")) and the [Edinburgh Annuale](/wiki/Edinburgh_Annuale "Edinburgh Annuale"). Many small private shops/galleries provide space to showcase works from local artists.[[219]](#cite_note-225)

### Shopping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Shopping")]

The locale around [Princes Street](/wiki/Princes_Street "Princes Street") is the main shopping area in the city centre, with souvenir shops, chain stores such as [Boots the Chemist](/wiki/Boots_the_Chemist "Boots the Chemist"), [Edinburgh Woollen Mill](/wiki/Edinburgh_Woollen_Mill "Edinburgh Woollen Mill"), and [H&M](/wiki/H%26M "H&M").[[220]](#cite_note-shopping-226) [George Street](/wiki/George_Street,_Edinburgh "George Street, Edinburgh"), north of Princes Street, has several upmarket shops and independent stores.[[220]](#cite_note-shopping-226) At the east end of Princes Street, the redeveloped [St James Quarter](/wiki/St_James_Quarter "St James Quarter") opened its doors in June 2021,[[221]](#cite_note-227) while next to the [Balmoral Hotel](/wiki/Balmoral_Hotel "Balmoral Hotel") and Waverley Station is [Waverley Market](/wiki/Waverley_Market "Waverley Market"). [Multrees Walk](/wiki/Multrees_Walk "Multrees Walk") is a pedestrian shopping district, dominated by the presence of [Harvey Nichols](/wiki/Harvey_Nichols "Harvey Nichols"), and other names including [Louis Vuitton](/wiki/Louis_Vuitton "Louis Vuitton"), [Mulberry](/wiki/Mulberry_(company) "Mulberry (company)") and [Michael Kors](/wiki/Michael_Kors "Michael Kors").[[220]](#cite_note-shopping-226)

Edinburgh also has substantial retail parks outside the city centre. These include [The Gyle Shopping Centre](/wiki/The_Gyle_Shopping_Centre "The Gyle Shopping Centre") and Hermiston Gait in the west of the city, [Cameron Toll Shopping Centre](/wiki/Cameron_Toll_Shopping_Centre "Cameron Toll Shopping Centre"), Straiton Retail Park (actually just outside the city, in Midlothian) and [Fort Kinnaird](/wiki/Fort_Kinnaird "Fort Kinnaird") in the south and east, and [Ocean Terminal](/wiki/Ocean_Terminal,_Edinburgh "Ocean Terminal, Edinburgh") in the north on the [Leith](/wiki/Leith "Leith") waterfront.[[222]](#cite_note-228)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Main articles: [Politics of Edinburgh](/wiki/Politics_of_Edinburgh "Politics of Edinburgh") and [City of Edinburgh Council](/wiki/City_of_Edinburgh_Council "City of Edinburgh Council")

### Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Government")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Edinburgh_-_2016_-_panoramio_-_StevenL_%289%29.jpg/250px-Edinburgh_-_2016_-_panoramio_-_StevenL_%289%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_-_2016_-_panoramio_-_StevenL_(9).jpg)

[Edinburgh City Chambers](/wiki/Edinburgh_City_Chambers "Edinburgh City Chambers") is the headquarters of the [City of Edinburgh Council](/wiki/City_of_Edinburgh_Council "City of Edinburgh Council").

Following local government reorganisation in 1996, the City of Edinburgh Council constitutes one of the [32 council areas of Scotland](/wiki/Council_areas_of_Scotland "Council areas of Scotland").[[223]](#cite_note-act-229) Like all other [local authorities of Scotland](/wiki/Local_authorities_of_Scotland "Local authorities of Scotland"), the council has powers over most matters of local administration such as housing, planning, [local transport](/wiki/Transport_in_Edinburgh "Transport in Edinburgh"), parks, economic development and regeneration.[[224]](#cite_note-act1-230) The council comprises 63 elected [councillors](/wiki/Councillor "Councillor"), returned from 17 [multi-member electoral wards](/wiki/Wards_of_the_United_Kingdom "Wards of the United Kingdom") in the city.[[225]](#cite_note-councillors-231) Following the [2007 City of Edinburgh Council election](/wiki/2007_City_of_Edinburgh_Council_election "2007 City of Edinburgh Council election") the incumbent [Labour Party](/wiki/Scottish_Labour "Scottish Labour") lost majority control of the council following 23 years to a [Liberal Democrat](/wiki/Scottish_Liberal_Democrats "Scottish Liberal Democrats")/[SNP](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") coalition.[[226]](#cite_note-232)

Following the 2017 election, the SNP and Labour formed a coalition administration, which lasted until the next election in 2022. The [2022 City of Edinburgh Council election](/wiki/2022_City_of_Edinburgh_Council_election "2022 City of Edinburgh Council election") resulted in the most politically balanced council in the UK, with 19 SNP, 13 Labour, 12 Liberal Democrat, 10 Green, and 9 Conservative councillors. A minority Labour administration was formed, being voted in by Scottish Conservative and Scottish Liberal Democrat councillors. The SNP and Greens presented a coalition agreement, but could not command a majority in the council. This caused controversy amongst the Scottish Labour Party group for forming an administration supported by Conservatives, and led to the suspension of two Labour councillors on the council for abstaining on the vote to approve the new administration.[[227]](#cite_note-233) The city's [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Edinburgh "Coat of arms of Edinburgh") was registered by the [Lord Lyon King of Arms](/wiki/Lord_Lyon_King_of_Arms "Lord Lyon King of Arms") in 1732.[[228]](#cite_note-234)

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Politics")]

[![Scottish Parliament](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/The_Scottish_Parliament_Building_-_geograph.org.uk_-_5190256.jpg/250px-The_Scottish_Parliament_Building_-_geograph.org.uk_-_5190256.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Scottish_Parliament_Building_-_geograph.org.uk_-_5190256.jpg)

The [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament") is located in the [Holyrood](/wiki/Holyrood_Park "Holyrood Park") area of the city.

[![St Andrew's House](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/St_Andrew%27s_House%2C_Edinburgh_%282496437377%29.jpg/250px-St_Andrew%27s_House%2C_Edinburgh_%282496437377%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Andrew%27s_House,_Edinburgh_(2496437377).jpg)

The HQ of the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") is located at [St Andrew's House](/wiki/St_Andrew%27s_House "St Andrew's House") in the city.

Edinburgh, like all of Scotland, is represented in the [Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Scottish_Parliament "Scottish Parliament"), situated in the [Holyrood](/wiki/Holyrood,_Edinburgh "Holyrood, Edinburgh") area of the city. For electoral purposes, the city is divided into six constituencies, which, along with 3 seats outside of the city, form part of the [Lothian region](/wiki/Lothian_(Scottish_Parliament_electoral_region) "Lothian (Scottish Parliament electoral region)").[[229]](#cite_note-elections-235) Each constituency elects one [Member of the Scottish Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_the_Scottish_Parliament "Member of the Scottish Parliament") (MSP) by the [first past the post](/wiki/First_past_the_post "First past the post") system of election, and the region elects seven [additional MSPs](/wiki/Additional_Member_System "Additional Member System") to produce a result based on a form of proportional representation.[[229]](#cite_note-elections-235)

As of December 2025, the Scottish National Party have three MSPs: [Ben Macpherson](/wiki/Ben_Macpherson_(politician) "Ben Macpherson (politician)") for [Edinburgh Northern and Leith](/wiki/Edinburgh_Northern_and_Leith_(Scottish_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh Northern and Leith (Scottish Parliament constituency)"), [Gordon MacDonald](/wiki/Gordon_MacDonald_(Scottish_politician) "Gordon MacDonald (Scottish politician)") for [Edinburgh Pentlands](/wiki/Edinburgh_Pentlands_(Scottish_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh Pentlands (Scottish Parliament constituency)") and [Angus Robertson](/wiki/Angus_Robertson "Angus Robertson") for [Edinburgh Central](/wiki/Edinburgh_Central_(Scottish_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh Central (Scottish Parliament constituency)") constituencies. [Alex Cole-Hamilton](/wiki/Alex_Cole-Hamilton "Alex Cole-Hamilton"), the leader of the [Scottish Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Scottish_Liberal_Democrats "Scottish Liberal Democrats") represents [Edinburgh Western](/wiki/Edinburgh_Western_(Scottish_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh Western (Scottish Parliament constituency)") and [Daniel Johnson](/wiki/Daniel_Johnson_(Scottish_politician) "Daniel Johnson (Scottish politician)") of the [Scottish Labour Party](/wiki/Scottish_Labour "Scottish Labour") represents [Edinburgh Southern](/wiki/Edinburgh_Southern_(Scottish_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh Southern (Scottish Parliament constituency)") constituency. [Ash Regan](/wiki/Ash_Regan "Ash Regan"), the MSP for [Edinburgh Eastern](/wiki/Edinburgh_Eastern_(Scottish_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh Eastern (Scottish Parliament constituency)"), currently sits as an [independent](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician"). In addition, the city is also represented by seven regional MSPs representing the Lothian electoral region: The Conservatives currently have two regional MSPs: [Miles Briggs](/wiki/Miles_Briggs "Miles Briggs") and [Sue Webber](/wiki/Sue_Webber "Sue Webber"), Labour have one regional MSP: [Sarah Boyack](/wiki/Sarah_Boyack "Sarah Boyack"), and two Scottish Green regional MSPs were elected: Green's former Co-Leader [Lorna Slater](/wiki/Lorna_Slater "Lorna Slater") and [Alison Johnstone](/wiki/Alison_Johnstone "Alison Johnstone"). [Jeremy Balfour](/wiki/Jeremy_Balfour "Jeremy Balfour") and [Foysol Choudhury](/wiki/Foysol_Choudhury "Foysol Choudhury") currently sit as independent regional MSPs.

Edinburgh is also represented in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") by five [Members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)"). The city is divided into [Edinburgh North and Leith](/wiki/Edinburgh_North_and_Leith_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh North and Leith (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Edinburgh East and Musselburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh_East_and_Musselburgh_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh East and Musselburgh (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Edinburgh South](/wiki/Edinburgh_South_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh South (UK Parliament constituency)"), [Edinburgh South West](/wiki/Edinburgh_South_West "Edinburgh South West"), and [Edinburgh West](/wiki/Edinburgh_West_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Edinburgh West (UK Parliament constituency)"), each constituency electing one member by the first past the post system. Since the [2024 General election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election "2024 United Kingdom general election"), Edinburgh is represented by four [Labour](/wiki/Scottish_Labour "Scottish Labour") MPs ([Tracy Gilbert](/wiki/Tracy_Gilbert "Tracy Gilbert") in Edinburgh North and Leith, [Chris Murray](/wiki/Chris_Murray_(politician) "Chris Murray (politician)") in Edinburgh East and Musselburgh, [Ian Murray](/wiki/Ian_Murray_(Scottish_politician) "Ian Murray (Scottish politician)") in Edinburgh South, and [Scott Arthur](/wiki/Scott_Arthur "Scott Arthur") in Edinburgh South West), and one [Liberal Democrat](/wiki/Scottish_Liberal_Democrats "Scottish Liberal Democrats") MP in Edinburgh West ([Christine Jardine](/wiki/Christine_Jardine "Christine Jardine")).

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main article: [Transport in Edinburgh](/wiki/Transport_in_Edinburgh "Transport in Edinburgh")

### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Air")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Edinburgh_Airport_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3588330.jpg/250px-Edinburgh_Airport_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3588330.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_Airport_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3588330.jpg)

[Edinburgh Airport](/wiki/Edinburgh_Airport "Edinburgh Airport") is the busiest airport in Scotland and [sixth busiest in the United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of busiest airports in the United Kingdom").

[Edinburgh Airport](/wiki/Edinburgh_Airport "Edinburgh Airport") is Scotland's busiest airport and the principal international gateway to the capital, handling over 14.7 million passengers; it was also the [sixth-busiest airport in the United Kingdom](/wiki/Busiest_airports_in_the_United_Kingdom_by_total_passenger_traffic "Busiest airports in the United Kingdom by total passenger traffic") by total passengers in 2019.[[230]](#cite_note-236)[[231]](#cite_note-237) In anticipation of rising passenger numbers, the former operator of the airport [BAA](/wiki/BAA_Limited "BAA Limited") outlined a draft masterplan in 2011 to provide for the expansion of the airfield and the terminal building. In June 2012, [Global Infrastructure Partners](/wiki/Global_Infrastructure_Partners "Global Infrastructure Partners") purchased the airport for £807 million.[[232]](#cite_note-238) The possibility of building a second runway to cope with an increased number of aircraft movements has also been mooted.[[233]](#cite_note-239)

### Buses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Buses")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Lothian_Buses_bus_769_%28SN56_ACJ%29%2C_24_June_2014.jpg/250px-Lothian_Buses_bus_769_%28SN56_ACJ%29%2C_24_June_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lothian_Buses_bus_769_(SN56_ACJ),_24_June_2014.jpg)

A Lothian Bus on [North Bridge](/wiki/North_Bridge,_Edinburgh "North Bridge, Edinburgh")

Travel in Edinburgh is undertaken predominantly by bus. [Lothian Buses](/wiki/Lothian_Buses "Lothian Buses"), the successor company to Edinburgh Corporation Transport Department, operates the majority of [city bus services](/wiki/Public_transport_bus_service "Public transport bus service") within the city and to surrounding towns and villages, with most routes running via Princes Street. Services further afield operate from the [Edinburgh Bus Station](/wiki/Edinburgh_Bus_Station "Edinburgh Bus Station") off [St Andrew Square](/wiki/St_Andrew_Square,_Edinburgh "St Andrew Square, Edinburgh") and Waterloo Place and are operated mainly by [Stagecoach East Scotland](/wiki/Stagecoach_East_Scotland "Stagecoach East Scotland"), [Scottish Citylink](/wiki/Scottish_Citylink "Scottish Citylink"), [National Express Coaches](/wiki/National_Express_Coaches "National Express Coaches") and [Borders Buses](/wiki/Borders_Buses "Borders Buses").

Lothian Buses and [McGill's Scotland East](/wiki/McGill%27s_Scotland_East "McGill's Scotland East") operate the city's branded public [tour buses](/wiki/Tour_bus_service "Tour bus service"). The [night bus](/wiki/Night_bus "Night bus") service and airport buses are mainly operated by Lothian Buses.[[234]](#cite_note-240) In 2019, Lothian Buses recorded 124.2 million passenger journeys.[[235]](#cite_note-241)

To tackle [traffic congestion](/wiki/Traffic_congestion "Traffic congestion"), Edinburgh is now served by six [park & ride](/wiki/Park_%26_ride "Park & ride") sites on the periphery of the city at Sheriffhall (in Midlothian), [Ingliston](/wiki/Ingliston "Ingliston"), [Riccarton](/wiki/Riccarton,_Edinburgh "Riccarton, Edinburgh"), [Inverkeithing](/wiki/Inverkeithing "Inverkeithing") (in Fife), [Newcraighall](/wiki/Newcraighall "Newcraighall") and [Straiton](/wiki/Straiton,_Loanhead "Straiton, Loanhead") (in Midlothian). A [referendum](/wiki/Edinburgh_congestion_charge#Referendum "Edinburgh congestion charge") of Edinburgh residents in February 2005 rejected a proposal to introduce [congestion charging](/wiki/Edinburgh_congestion_charge "Edinburgh congestion charge") in the city.
[[236]](#cite_note-242)

### Railway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Railway")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/334038_sits_at_Edinburgh_Waverley%2C_05_April_2013.JPG/250px-334038_sits_at_Edinburgh_Waverley%2C_05_April_2013.JPG)](/wiki/File:334038_sits_at_Edinburgh_Waverley,_05_April_2013.JPG)

A [Class 334](/wiki/British_Rail_Class_334 "British Rail Class 334") train preparing to depart from Edinburgh Waverley station

[Edinburgh Waverley](/wiki/Edinburgh_Waverley_railway_station "Edinburgh Waverley railway station") is the second-busiest railway station in Scotland, with only [Glasgow Central](/wiki/Glasgow_Central_railway_station "Glasgow Central railway station") handling more passengers. On the evidence of passenger entries and exits between April 2015 and March 2016, Edinburgh Waverley is the fifth-busiest station outside London; it is also the UK's second biggest station in terms of the number of platforms and area size.[[237]](#cite_note-243) Waverley is the terminus for most trains arriving from [London King's Cross](/wiki/London_King%27s_Cross_railway_station "London King's Cross railway station") and the departure point for many [rail services within Scotland](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Scotland "Rail transport in Scotland") operated by [ScotRail](/wiki/ScotRail "ScotRail").

To the west of the city centre lies [Haymarket station](/wiki/Haymarket_railway_station "Haymarket railway station"), which is an important commuter stop. Opened in 2003, [Edinburgh Park](/wiki/Edinburgh_Park_railway_station "Edinburgh Park railway station") station serves the Gyle business park in the west of the city and the nearby [Gogarburn](/wiki/Gogarburn "Gogarburn") headquarters of the Royal Bank of Scotland. The [Edinburgh Crossrail](/wiki/Edinburgh_Crossrail "Edinburgh Crossrail") route connects Edinburgh Park with Haymarket, Edinburgh Waverley and the suburban stations of [Brunstane](/wiki/Brunstane_railway_station "Brunstane railway station") and [Newcraighall](/wiki/Newcraighall_railway_station "Newcraighall railway station") in the east of the city.[[238]](#cite_note-244) There are also commuter lines to [Edinburgh Gateway](/wiki/Edinburgh_Gateway "Edinburgh Gateway"), [South Gyle](/wiki/South_Gyle "South Gyle") and Dalmeny, the latter serving [South Queensferry](/wiki/South_Queensferry "South Queensferry") by the Forth Bridges, and to [Wester Hailes](/wiki/Wester_Hailes "Wester Hailes") and [Curriehill](/wiki/Curriehill "Curriehill") in the south-west of the city.

### Trams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Trams")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Edinburgh_trams%2C_Shandwick_Place.JPG/250px-Edinburgh_trams%2C_Shandwick_Place.JPG)](/wiki/File:Edinburgh_trams,_Shandwick_Place.JPG)

Edinburgh Trams in Shandwick Place

[Edinburgh Trams](/wiki/Edinburgh_Trams "Edinburgh Trams") became operational on 31 May 2014. The city had been without a tram system since [Edinburgh Corporation Tramways](/wiki/Edinburgh_Corporation_Tramways "Edinburgh Corporation Tramways") ceased on 16 November 1956.[[239]](#cite_note-245) Following parliamentary approval in 2007, construction began in early 2008. The first stage of the project was expected to be completed by July 2011[[240]](#cite_note-operational-246) but, following delays caused by extra utility work and a long-running contractual dispute between the council and the main contractor, [Bilfinger SE](/wiki/Bilfinger_SE "Bilfinger SE"), the project was rescheduled.[[241]](#cite_note-247)[[242]](#cite_note-248)[[243]](#cite_note-249) The line opened in 2014 but had been cut short to 8.7 mi (14.0 km) in length, running from [Edinburgh Airport](/wiki/Edinburgh_Airport_tram_stop "Edinburgh Airport tram stop") to [York Place](/wiki/York_Place,_Edinburgh "York Place, Edinburgh") in the east end of the city.

The line was later extended north onto [Leith](/wiki/Leith "Leith") and [Newhaven](/wiki/Newhaven,_Edinburgh "Newhaven, Edinburgh"), opening a further eight stops to passengers in June 2023. The York Place stop was replaced by a new island stop at [Picardy Place](/wiki/Greenside,_Edinburgh "Greenside, Edinburgh"). The original plan would have seen a second line run from Haymarket through [Ravelston](/wiki/Ravelston "Ravelston") and [Craigleith](/wiki/Craigleith,_Edinburgh "Craigleith, Edinburgh") to [Granton Square](/wiki/Granton,_Edinburgh "Granton, Edinburgh") on the [Waterfront Edinburgh](/wiki/Waterfront_Edinburgh "Waterfront Edinburgh"). This was shelved in 2011 but in 2025 is once again [under consideration](/wiki/Proposals_for_new_tram_lines_in_Edinburgh "Proposals for new tram lines in Edinburgh"), as is another line potentially linking the south of the city and the [Bioquarter](/wiki/Edinburgh_BioQuarter "Edinburgh BioQuarter").[[244]](#cite_note-250) There were also long-term plans for lines running west from the airport to [Ratho](/wiki/Ratho "Ratho") and [Newbridge](/wiki/Newbridge,_Edinburgh "Newbridge, Edinburgh") and another connecting [Granton](/wiki/Granton,_Edinburgh "Granton, Edinburgh") to Newhaven via Lower Granton Road. In January 2026, a map showing a [potential future network](/wiki/Proposals_for_new_tram_lines_in_Edinburgh#Other_proposals:_Edinburgh_South_Suburban_Railway "Proposals for new tram lines in Edinburgh") using the [Edinburgh South Suburban Railway](/wiki/Edinburgh_South_Suburban_Railway "Edinburgh South Suburban Railway") was published by the council.[[245]](#cite_note-:22-251) Lothian Buses and Edinburgh Trams are both owned and operated by [Transport for Edinburgh](/wiki/Transport_for_Edinburgh "Transport for Edinburgh").

Despite its modern transport links, in January 2021, Edinburgh was named the most congested city in the UK for the fourth year running, though it has since fallen to 7th place in 2022.[[246]](#cite_note-252)[[247]](#cite_note-253)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Education")]

### Schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Schools")]

There are 18 nursery, 94 primary and 23 secondary [schools](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_Edinburgh "List of schools in Edinburgh") administered by the City of Edinburgh Council.[[248]](#cite_note-primaryschools-254)
Edinburgh is home to [The Royal High School](/wiki/Royal_High_School,_Edinburgh "Royal High School, Edinburgh"), one of the [oldest schools in the country](/wiki/List_of_the_oldest_schools_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of the oldest schools in the United Kingdom") and [the world](/wiki/List_of_the_oldest_schools_in_the_world "List of the oldest schools in the world"). The city also has several [independent, fee-paying schools](/wiki/Independent_school_(UK)#Independent_schools_in_Scotland "Independent school (UK)") including [Edinburgh Academy](/wiki/Edinburgh_Academy "Edinburgh Academy"), [Fettes College](/wiki/Fettes_College "Fettes College"), [George Heriot's School](/wiki/George_Heriot%27s_School "George Heriot's School"), [George Watson's College](/wiki/George_Watson%27s_College "George Watson's College"), [Merchiston Castle School](/wiki/Merchiston_Castle_School "Merchiston Castle School"), [Stewart's Melville College](/wiki/Stewart%27s_Melville_College "Stewart's Melville College") and [The Mary Erskine School](/wiki/The_Mary_Erskine_School "The Mary Erskine School"). In 2009, the proportion of pupils attending independent schools was 24.2%, far above the Scottish national average of just over 7% and higher than in any other region of Scotland.[[249]](#cite_note-SCISstats-255) In August 2013, the City of Edinburgh Council opened the city's first stand-alone Gaelic primary school, [Bun-sgoil Taobh na Pàirce](/wiki/Bun-sgoil_Taobh_na_P%C3%A0irce "Bun-sgoil Taobh na Pàirce").[[250]](#cite_note-256)

### College and university

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: College and university")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Old_College%2C_Edinburgh_University.jpg/250px-Old_College%2C_Edinburgh_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_College,_Edinburgh_University.jpg)

[Old College](/wiki/Old_College,_Edinburgh "Old College, Edinburgh") of the University of Edinburgh

There are three universities in Edinburgh: the [University of Edinburgh](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh "University of Edinburgh"), [Heriot-Watt University](/wiki/Heriot-Watt_University "Heriot-Watt University"), and [Edinburgh Napier University](/wiki/Edinburgh_Napier_University "Edinburgh Napier University"). Established by royal charter in 1583, the University of Edinburgh is one of Scotland's [ancient universities](/wiki/Ancient_universities_of_Scotland "Ancient universities of Scotland") and is the fourth oldest in the country after [St Andrews](/wiki/University_of_St_Andrews "University of St Andrews"), [Glasgow](/wiki/University_of_Glasgow "University of Glasgow") and [Aberdeen](/wiki/University_of_Aberdeen "University of Aberdeen").[[251]](#cite_note-Universityhistory-257) Originally centred on [Old College](/wiki/Old_College,_University_of_Edinburgh "Old College, University of Edinburgh") the university expanded to premises on The Mound, the Royal Mile and George Square.[[251]](#cite_note-Universityhistory-257) Today, the [King's Buildings](/wiki/King%27s_Buildings "King's Buildings") in the south of the city contain most of the schools within the College of Science and Engineering. In 2002, the [medical school](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh_Medical_School "University of Edinburgh Medical School") moved to purpose-built accommodation adjacent to the new [Royal Infirmary of Edinburgh](/wiki/Royal_Infirmary_of_Edinburgh "Royal Infirmary of Edinburgh") at [Little France](/wiki/Little_France "Little France"). The university was placed 16th in the QS World University Rankings for 2022.[[252]](#cite_note-University_Rankings-258)

Heriot-Watt University is based at the [Riccarton](/wiki/Riccarton,_Edinburgh "Riccarton, Edinburgh") campus in the west of Edinburgh. Originally established in 1821 as the world's first [mechanics' institute](/wiki/Mechanics%27_Institutes "Mechanics' Institutes"), it was granted university status by royal charter in 1966. It has other campuses in the Scottish Borders, Orkney, the United Arab Emirates and Putrajaya in Malaysia. It takes the name *Heriot-Watt* from Scottish inventor [James Watt](/wiki/James_Watt "James Watt") and Scottish philanthropist and goldsmith [George Heriot](/wiki/George_Heriot "George Heriot"). Heriot-Watt University has been named International University of the Year by *The Times and Sunday Times Good University Guide 2018*. In the latest Research Excellence Framework, it was ranked overall in the Top 25% of UK universities and 1st in Scotland for research impact.

Edinburgh Napier University was originally founded as Napier College, which was renamed Napier Polytechnic in 1986 and gained university status in 1992.[[253]](#cite_note-Napierhistory-259) Edinburgh Napier University has campuses in the south and west of the city, including the former [Merchiston Tower](/wiki/Merchiston_Tower "Merchiston Tower") and [Craiglockhart Hydropathic](/wiki/Craiglockhart_Hydropathic "Craiglockhart Hydropathic").[[253]](#cite_note-Napierhistory-259) It is home to the [Screen Academy Scotland](/wiki/Screen_Academy_Scotland "Screen Academy Scotland"). [Queen Margaret University](/wiki/Queen_Margaret_University "Queen Margaret University") was located in Edinburgh before it moved outside the city boundary to a new campus in the county of [East Lothian](/wiki/East_Lothian "East Lothian") on the outskirts of [Musselburgh](/wiki/Musselburgh "Musselburgh") in 2008.[[254]](#cite_note-260) Until 2012, further education colleges in the city included [Jewel and Esk College](/wiki/Jewel_and_Esk_College "Jewel and Esk College") (incorporating [Leith Nautical College](/wiki/Leith_Nautical_College "Leith Nautical College") founded in 1903), [Telford College](/wiki/Edinburgh%27s_Telford_College "Edinburgh's Telford College"), opened in 1968, and [Stevenson College](/wiki/Stevenson_College_(Edinburgh) "Stevenson College (Edinburgh)"), opened in 1970. These have now been amalgamated to form [Edinburgh College](/wiki/Edinburgh_College "Edinburgh College"). [Scotland's Rural College](/wiki/Scotland%27s_Rural_College "Scotland's Rural College") also has a campus in South Edinburgh. Other institutions include the [Royal College of Surgeons of Edinburgh](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Surgeons_of_Edinburgh "Royal College of Surgeons of Edinburgh") and the [Royal College of Physicians of Edinburgh](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Physicians_of_Edinburgh "Royal College of Physicians of Edinburgh"), which were established by royal charter in 1506 and 1681, respectively. The [Trustees' Academy](/wiki/Trustees%27_Academy "Trustees' Academy") of Edinburgh, founded in 1760, became the [Edinburgh College of Art](/wiki/Edinburgh_College_of_Art "Edinburgh College of Art") in 1907.[[255]](#cite_note-261)

## Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/EdinburghRoyalInfirmary.jpg/250px-EdinburghRoyalInfirmary.jpg)](/wiki/File:EdinburghRoyalInfirmary.jpg)

The Royal Infirmary of Edinburgh is the main public hospital for the city.

See also: [List of hospitals in Edinburgh](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_Scotland#City_of_Edinburgh "List of hospitals in Scotland")

The main [NHS Lothian](/wiki/NHS_Lothian "NHS Lothian") hospitals serving the Edinburgh area are the [Royal Infirmary of Edinburgh](/wiki/Royal_Infirmary_of_Edinburgh "Royal Infirmary of Edinburgh"), which includes the [University of Edinburgh Medical School](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh_Medical_School "University of Edinburgh Medical School"), and the [Western General Hospital](/wiki/Western_General_Hospital "Western General Hospital"),[[256]](#cite_note-hospitals-262) which has a large cancer treatment centre and nurse-led Minor Injuries Clinic.[[257]](#cite_note-263) The [Royal Edinburgh Hospital](/wiki/Royal_Edinburgh_Hospital "Royal Edinburgh Hospital") in Morningside specialises in mental health. The [Royal Hospital for Children and Young People](/wiki/Royal_Hospital_for_Children_and_Young_People "Royal Hospital for Children and Young People"), colloquially referred to as *the Sick Kids*, is a specialist [paediatrics](/wiki/Paediatrics "Paediatrics") hospital.

There are two private hospitals: Murrayfield Hospital in the west of the city and Shawfair Hospital in the south; both are owned by [Spire Healthcare](/wiki/Spire_Healthcare "Spire Healthcare").[[256]](#cite_note-hospitals-262)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Sport")]

### Football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Football")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Tynecastle_Park%2C_January_2018.jpg/250px-Tynecastle_Park%2C_January_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tynecastle_Park,_January_2018.jpg)

[Tynecastle Park](/wiki/Tynecastle_Park "Tynecastle Park")

Edinburgh has four [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") clubs that play in the [Scottish Professional Football League](/wiki/Scottish_Professional_Football_League "Scottish Professional Football League") (SPFL): [Heart of Midlothian](/wiki/Heart_of_Midlothian_F.C. "Heart of Midlothian F.C."), founded in 1874, [Hibernian](/wiki/Hibernian_F.C. "Hibernian F.C."), founded in 1875, [Edinburgh City F.C.](/wiki/Edinburgh_City_F.C. "Edinburgh City F.C."), founded in 1966 and [Spartans](/wiki/Spartans_F.C. "Spartans F.C."), founded in 1951. Heart of Midlothian and Hibernian are known locally as "Hearts" and "Hibs", respectively. Both play in the [Scottish Premiership](/wiki/Scottish_Premiership "Scottish Premiership").[[258]](#cite_note-264) They are the oldest city rivals in Scotland and the [Edinburgh derby](/wiki/Edinburgh_derby "Edinburgh derby") is one of the oldest derby matches in world football. Both clubs have won the [Scottish league championship](/wiki/List_of_Scottish_football_champions "List of Scottish football champions") four times. Hearts have won the [Scottish Cup](/wiki/Scottish_Cup "Scottish Cup") eight times and the [Scottish League Cup](/wiki/Scottish_League_Cup "Scottish League Cup") four times. Hibs have won the Scottish Cup and the Scottish League Cup three times each. [Edinburgh City](/wiki/Edinburgh_City_F.C. "Edinburgh City F.C.") were promoted to [Scottish League Two](/wiki/Scottish_League_Two "Scottish League Two") in the 2015–16 season, becoming the first club to win promotion to the SPFL via the pyramid system playoffs.

Edinburgh was also home to four other [former Scottish Football League clubs](/wiki/List_of_former_Scottish_Football_League_clubs "List of former Scottish Football League clubs"): the original [Edinburgh City](/wiki/Edinburgh_City_F.C._(1928) "Edinburgh City F.C. (1928)") (founded in 1928), [Leith Athletic](/wiki/Leith_Athletic_F.C. "Leith Athletic F.C."), [Meadowbank Thistle](/wiki/Meadowbank_Thistle_F.C. "Meadowbank Thistle F.C.") and [St Bernard's](/wiki/St_Bernard%27s_F.C. "St Bernard's F.C."). Meadowbank Thistle played at [Meadowbank Stadium](/wiki/Meadowbank_Stadium "Meadowbank Stadium") until 1995, when the club moved to [Livingston](/wiki/Livingston,_West_Lothian "Livingston, West Lothian") and became [Livingston F.C](/wiki/Livingston_F.C. "Livingston F.C."). The [Scottish national team](/wiki/Scotland_men%27s_national_football_team "Scotland men's national football team") has very occasionally played at [Easter Road](/wiki/Easter_Road "Easter Road") and [Tynecastle](/wiki/Tynecastle_Park "Tynecastle Park"), although its normal [home stadium](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team_home_stadium "Scotland national football team home stadium") is [Hampden Park](/wiki/Hampden_Park "Hampden Park") in Glasgow. St Bernard's [New Logie Green](/wiki/New_Logie_Green "New Logie Green") was used to host the [1896 Scottish Cup Final](/wiki/1896_Scottish_Cup_Final "1896 Scottish Cup Final"), the only time the match has been played outside Glasgow.[[259]](#cite_note-UD-265) The city also plays host to [Lowland Football League](/wiki/Lowland_Football_League "Lowland Football League") clubs [Civil Service Strollers](/wiki/Civil_Service_Strollers_F.C. "Civil Service Strollers F.C."), [Edinburgh University](/wiki/Edinburgh_University_A.F.C. "Edinburgh University A.F.C.") and [Spartans](/wiki/Spartans_F.C. "Spartans F.C."), as well as [East of Scotland League](/wiki/East_of_Scotland_Football_League "East of Scotland Football League") clubs [Craigroyston](/wiki/Craigroyston_F.C. "Craigroyston F.C."), [Edinburgh United](/wiki/Edinburgh_United_F.C. "Edinburgh United F.C."), [Heriot-Watt University](/wiki/Heriot-Watt_University_F.C. "Heriot-Watt University F.C."), [Leith Athletic](/wiki/Leith_Athletic_F.C. "Leith Athletic F.C."), [Lothian Thistle Hutchison Vale](/wiki/Lothian_Thistle_Hutchison_Vale_F.C. "Lothian Thistle Hutchison Vale F.C."), and [Tynecastle](/wiki/Tynecastle_F.C. "Tynecastle F.C.").

In women's football, [Hearts](/wiki/Hearts_Ladies_F.C. "Hearts Ladies F.C."), [Hibs](/wiki/Hibernian_L.F.C. "Hibernian L.F.C.") and [Spartans](/wiki/Spartans_W.F.C. "Spartans W.F.C.") play in the [SWPL 1](/wiki/Scottish_Women%27s_Premier_League "Scottish Women's Premier League").[[260]](#cite_note-266) [Hutchison Vale](/wiki/Lothian_Thistle_Hutchison_Vale_F.C.#Women "Lothian Thistle Hutchison Vale F.C.") and [Boroughmuir Thistle](/wiki/Boroughmuir_Thistle_F.C. "Boroughmuir Thistle F.C.") play in the [SWPL 2](/wiki/Scottish_Women%27s_Premier_League "Scottish Women's Premier League").[[261]](#cite_note-267)

### Rugby

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Rugby")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Murrayfield_Autumn_2017.jpg/250px-Murrayfield_Autumn_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Murrayfield_Autumn_2017.jpg)

[Murrayfield Stadium](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium")

The [Scotland national rugby union team](/wiki/Scotland_national_rugby_union_team "Scotland national rugby union team") play at [Murrayfield Stadium](/wiki/Murrayfield_Stadium "Murrayfield Stadium"), and the professional [Edinburgh Rugby](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby "Edinburgh Rugby") team play at the nextdoor [Edinburgh Rugby Stadium](/wiki/Edinburgh_Rugby_Stadium "Edinburgh Rugby Stadium"); both are owned by the [Scottish Rugby Union](/wiki/Scottish_Rugby_Union "Scottish Rugby Union") and are also used for other events, including music concerts. Murrayfield is the largest capacity stadium in Scotland, seating 67,144 spectators.[[262]](#cite_note-268) Edinburgh is also home to [Scottish Premiership](/wiki/Scottish_Premiership_(rugby) "Scottish Premiership (rugby)") teams [Boroughmuir RFC](/wiki/Boroughmuir_RFC "Boroughmuir RFC"), [Currie RFC](/wiki/Currie_RFC "Currie RFC"), the [Edinburgh Academicals](/wiki/Edinburgh_Academicals "Edinburgh Academicals"), [Heriot's Rugby Club](/wiki/Heriot%27s_Rugby_Club "Heriot's Rugby Club") and [Watsonians RFC](/wiki/Watsonians_RFC "Watsonians RFC").[[263]](#cite_note-269)

The Edinburgh Academicals ground at [Raeburn Place](/wiki/Raeburn_Place "Raeburn Place") was the location of the world's first international rugby game on 27 March 1871, between Scotland and England.[[264]](#cite_note-270) [Rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") is represented by the [Edinburgh Eagles](/wiki/Edinburgh_Eagles "Edinburgh Eagles") who play in the [Rugby League Conference Scotland Division](/wiki/Rugby_League_Conference_Scotland_Division "Rugby League Conference Scotland Division"). Murrayfield Stadium has hosted the [Magic Weekend](/wiki/Magic_Weekend "Magic Weekend") where all [Super League](/wiki/Super_League "Super League") matches are played in the stadium over one weekend.

### Other sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Other sports")]

The [Scottish cricket team](/wiki/Scottish_cricket_team "Scottish cricket team"), which represents Scotland internationally, plays its home matches at the [Grange cricket club](/wiki/The_Grange_Club "The Grange Club").[[265]](#cite_note-271) The [Edinburgh Capitals](/wiki/Edinburgh_Capitals "Edinburgh Capitals") are the latest of a succession of [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") clubs in the Scottish capital. Previously, Edinburgh was represented by the [Murrayfield Racers (2018)](/wiki/Murrayfield_Racers_(2018) "Murrayfield Racers (2018)"), the original [Murrayfield Racers](/wiki/Murrayfield_Racers "Murrayfield Racers") *(who folded in 1996)*, and the Edinburgh Racers. The club plays their home games at the [Murrayfield Ice Rink](/wiki/Murrayfield_Ice_Rink "Murrayfield Ice Rink") and have competed in the eleven-team professional [Scottish National League (SNL)](/wiki/Scottish_National_League_(ice_hockey) "Scottish National League (ice hockey)") since the 2018–19 season.[[266]](#cite_note-272)

[Caledonia Pride](/wiki/Caledonia_Pride "Caledonia Pride") is the only women's professional basketball team in Scotland. Established in 2016, the team competes in the UK-wide [Women's British Basketball League](/wiki/Women%27s_British_Basketball_League "Women's British Basketball League") and plays their home matches at the [Oriam](/wiki/Oriam "Oriam") National Performance Centre. Edinburgh also has several men's basketball teams within the Scottish National League. [Boroughmuir Blaze](/wiki/Boroughmuir_Blaze "Boroughmuir Blaze"), [City of Edinburgh Kings](/wiki/City_of_Edinburgh_Kings "City of Edinburgh Kings"), and [Edinburgh Lions](/wiki/Edinburgh_Lions "Edinburgh Lions") all compete in Division 1 of the National League, and [Pleasance B.C.](/wiki/Pleasance_B.C. "Pleasance B.C.") compete in Division 2. The [Edinburgh Diamond Devils](/wiki/Edinburgh_Diamond_Devils "Edinburgh Diamond Devils") is a baseball club that won its first Scottish Championship in 1991 as the "Reivers". 1992 saw the team repeat the achievement, becoming the first team to do so in league history. The same year saw the start of their first youth team, the Blue Jays. The club adopted its present name in 1999.[[267]](#cite_note-273)

Edinburgh has also hosted national and international sports events including the [World Student Games](/wiki/World_Student_Games "World Student Games"), the [1970 British Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1970_British_Commonwealth_Games "1970 British Commonwealth Games"),[[268]](#cite_note-cwgs-274) the [1986 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1986_Commonwealth_Games "1986 Commonwealth Games")[[268]](#cite_note-cwgs-274) and the inaugural 2000 Commonwealth Youth Games.[[269]](#cite_note-275) For the 1970 Games the city built Olympic standard venues and facilities including Meadowbank Stadium and the [Royal Commonwealth Pool](/wiki/Royal_Commonwealth_Pool "Royal Commonwealth Pool"). The Pool underwent refurbishment in 2012 and hosted the Diving competition in the [2014 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/2014_Commonwealth_Games "2014 Commonwealth Games"), which were held in Glasgow.[[270]](#cite_note-276)

The [New Year Sprint](/wiki/New_Year_Sprint "New Year Sprint") is a athletics sprint handicap race over 120 metres has been run continuously since 1870.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football"), the [Scottish Claymores](/wiki/Scottish_Claymores "Scottish Claymores") played [WLAF](/wiki/World_League_of_American_Football "World League of American Football")/[NFL Europe](/wiki/NFL_Europe "NFL Europe") games at Murrayfield, including their [World Bowl 96](/wiki/World_Bowl_IV "World Bowl IV") victory. From 1995 to 1997, they played all their games there, from 1998 to 2000 they split their home matches between Murrayfield and Glasgow's Hampden Park, then moved to Glasgow full-time, with one final Murrayfield appearance in 2002.[[271]](#cite_note-277) The city's most successful non-professional team are the [Edinburgh Wolves](/wiki/Edinburgh_Wolves "Edinburgh Wolves") who play at Meadowbank Stadium.[[272]](#cite_note-278) The [Edinburgh Marathon](/wiki/Edinburgh_Marathon "Edinburgh Marathon") has been held annually in the city since 2003 with more than 16,000 runners taking part on each occasion.[[273]](#cite_note-279) Its organisers have called it "the fastest marathon in the UK" due to the elevation drop of 40 m (130 ft).[[274]](#cite_note-280) The city also organises a half-marathon, as well as [10 km](/wiki/10,000_metres "10,000 metres") (6.2 mi) and [5 km](/wiki/5000_metres "5000 metres") (3.1 mi) races, including a 5 km (3 mi) race on 1 January each year.

Edinburgh has a [speedway](/wiki/Motorcycle_speedway "Motorcycle speedway") team, the [Edinburgh Monarchs](/wiki/Edinburgh_Monarchs "Edinburgh Monarchs"), which, since the loss of its stadium in the city, has raced at the Lothian Arena in [Armadale, West Lothian](/wiki/Armadale,_West_Lothian "Armadale, West Lothian"). The Monarchs have won the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League_(speedway) "Premier League (speedway)") championship five times in their history, in 2003[[275]](#cite_note-281) and again in 2008,[[276]](#cite_note-282) 2010, 2014 and 2015. Edinburgh also has Scotland's first onshore artificial open air [surfing pool](/wiki/Lost_Shore_Surf_Resort "Lost Shore Surf Resort"), located at former Craigpark quarry in [Ratho](/wiki/Ratho "Ratho").[[277]](#cite_note-283)

## People

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: People")]

Main article: [List of people from Edinburgh](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Edinburgh "List of people from Edinburgh")

See also: [List of University of Edinburgh people](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Edinburgh_people "List of University of Edinburgh people")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Sir_Walter_Scott_-_Raeburn-2.jpg/250px-Sir_Walter_Scott_-_Raeburn-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Walter_Scott_-_Raeburn-2.jpg)

Sir [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott "Walter Scott")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Greyfriars_Bobby_statue%2C_Edinburgh.JPG/250px-Greyfriars_Bobby_statue%2C_Edinburgh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Greyfriars_Bobby_statue,_Edinburgh.JPG)

[Greyfriars Bobby Fountain](/wiki/Greyfriars_Bobby_Fountain "Greyfriars Bobby Fountain")

Edinburgh has a literary tradition, which became especially evident during the [Scottish Enlightenment](/wiki/Scottish_Enlightenment "Scottish Enlightenment"). This heritage and the city's literary life in the present led to it being declared the first [UNESCO City of Literature](/wiki/UNESCO_City_of_Literature "UNESCO City of Literature") in 2004.[[278]](#cite_note-Urbanism-284)[[279]](#cite_note-285) Authors who have lived in Edinburgh include the economist [Adam Smith](/wiki/Adam_Smith "Adam Smith"), born in [Kirkcaldy](/wiki/Kirkcaldy "Kirkcaldy") and author of *[The Wealth of Nations](/wiki/The_Wealth_of_Nations "The Wealth of Nations")*,[[280]](#cite_note-famouspeoplecouncil-286) [James Boswell](/wiki/James_Boswell "James Boswell"), biographer of [Samuel Johnson](/wiki/Life_of_Samuel_Johnson "Life of Samuel Johnson"); Sir [Walter Scott](/wiki/Walter_Scott "Walter Scott"), creator of the historical novel and author of works such as *[Rob Roy](/wiki/Rob_Roy_(novel) "Rob Roy (novel)")*, *[Ivanhoe](/wiki/Ivanhoe "Ivanhoe")*, and *[Heart of Midlothian](/wiki/The_Heart_of_Midlothian "The Heart of Midlothian")*; [James Hogg](/wiki/James_Hogg "James Hogg"), author of *[The Private Memoirs and Confessions of a Justified Sinner](/wiki/The_Private_Memoirs_and_Confessions_of_a_Justified_Sinner "The Private Memoirs and Confessions of a Justified Sinner")*; [Robert Louis Stevenson](/wiki/Robert_Louis_Stevenson "Robert Louis Stevenson"),[[281]](#cite_note-287) creator of *[Treasure Island](/wiki/Treasure_Island "Treasure Island")*, *[Kidnapped](/wiki/Kidnapped_(novel) "Kidnapped (novel)")*, and *[Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde](/wiki/Strange_Case_of_Dr_Jekyll_and_Mr_Hyde "Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde")*; [Sir Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Arthur Conan Doyle"), the creator of [Sherlock Holmes](/wiki/Sherlock_Holmes "Sherlock Holmes"); [Muriel Spark](/wiki/Muriel_Spark "Muriel Spark"), author of *[The Prime of Miss Jean Brodie](/wiki/The_Prime_of_Miss_Jean_Brodie_(novel) "The Prime of Miss Jean Brodie (novel)")*; [Irvine Welsh](/wiki/Irvine_Welsh "Irvine Welsh"), author of *[Trainspotting](/wiki/Trainspotting_(novel) "Trainspotting (novel)")*, whose novels are mostly set in the city and often written in colloquial [Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language");
[[282]](#cite_note-288) [Ian Rankin](/wiki/Ian_Rankin "Ian Rankin"), author of the [Inspector Rebus](/wiki/Detective_Inspector_John_Rebus "Detective Inspector John Rebus") series of crime thrillers, [Alexander McCall Smith](/wiki/Alexander_McCall_Smith "Alexander McCall Smith"), author of the [No. 1 Ladies' Detective Agency](/wiki/No._1_Ladies%27_Detective_Agency "No. 1 Ladies' Detective Agency") series,[[283]](#cite_note-289) and [J. K. Rowling](/wiki/J._K._Rowling "J. K. Rowling"), author of [Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter "Harry Potter"), who moved to the city in 1993 and wrote much of her first book in Edinburgh coffee shops.[[284]](#cite_note-290)

Edinburgh produced figures in science and engineering. [John Napier](/wiki/John_Napier "John Napier"), inventor of [logarithms](/wiki/Logarithm "Logarithm"), was born in [Merchiston Tower](/wiki/Merchiston_Tower "Merchiston Tower") and lived and died in the city.[[285]](#cite_note-291) His house now forms part of the original campus of Napier University which was named in his honour. He lies buried under St. Cuthbert's Church. [James Clerk Maxwell](/wiki/James_Clerk_Maxwell "James Clerk Maxwell"), founder of the modern theory of [electromagnetism](/wiki/Electromagnetism "Electromagnetism"), was born at 14 India Street (now the home of the [James Clerk Maxwell Foundation](/wiki/James_Clerk_Maxwell_Foundation "James Clerk Maxwell Foundation")) and educated at the [Edinburgh Academy](/wiki/Edinburgh_Academy "Edinburgh Academy") and the University of Edinburgh,[[280]](#cite_note-famouspeoplecouncil-286) as was the engineer and telephone pioneer [Alexander Graham Bell](/wiki/Alexander_Graham_Bell "Alexander Graham Bell").[[280]](#cite_note-famouspeoplecouncil-286) [James Braidwood](/wiki/James_Braidwood_(firefighter) "James Braidwood (firefighter)"), who organised Britain's first municipal fire brigade, was also born in the city and began his career there.

Other names connected with the city include physicist [Max Born](/wiki/Max_Born "Max Born"), a principle founder of [Quantum mechanics](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics") and [Nobel laureate](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics");[[286]](#cite_note-292) [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin"), the biologist who propounded the theory of [natural selection](/wiki/Natural_selection "Natural selection");[[280]](#cite_note-famouspeoplecouncil-286) [David Hume](/wiki/David_Hume "David Hume"), philosopher, economist and historian;[[280]](#cite_note-famouspeoplecouncil-286) [James Hutton](/wiki/James_Hutton "James Hutton"), regarded as the "Father of Geology";[[280]](#cite_note-famouspeoplecouncil-286) [Joseph Black](/wiki/Joseph_Black "Joseph Black"), the chemist who discovered [magnesium](/wiki/Magnesium "Magnesium") and [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide"), and one of the founders of [Thermodynamics](/wiki/Thermodynamics "Thermodynamics");[[280]](#cite_note-famouspeoplecouncil-286) pioneering medical researchers [Joseph Lister](/wiki/Joseph_Lister,_1st_Baron_Lister "Joseph Lister, 1st Baron Lister") and [James Young Simpson](/wiki/James_Young_Simpson "James Young Simpson");[[280]](#cite_note-famouspeoplecouncil-286) chemist and discoverer of the element [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") [Daniel Rutherford](/wiki/Daniel_Rutherford "Daniel Rutherford"); [Colin Maclaurin](/wiki/Colin_Maclaurin "Colin Maclaurin"), mathematician and developer of the [Maclaurin series](/wiki/Maclaurin_series "Maclaurin series"),[[287]](#cite_note-293) and [Ian Wilmut](/wiki/Ian_Wilmut "Ian Wilmut"), the geneticist involved in the cloning of [Dolly the sheep](/wiki/Dolly_(sheep) "Dolly (sheep)") just outside Edinburgh, at the [Roslin Institute](/wiki/Roslin_Institute "Roslin Institute").[[280]](#cite_note-famouspeoplecouncil-286) The stuffed carcass of Dolly the sheep is now on display in the National Museum of Scotland.[[288]](#cite_note-294) The latest in a long line of science celebrities associated with the city is theoretical physicist, [Nobel laureate](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics") and professor emeritus at the [University of Edinburgh](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh "University of Edinburgh") [Peter Higgs](/wiki/Peter_Higgs "Peter Higgs"), born in Newcastle but resident in Edinburgh for most of his academic career, after whom the [Higgs boson](/wiki/Higgs_boson "Higgs boson") particle has been named.[[289]](#cite_note-295)

Edinburgh has been the birthplace of actors like [Alastair Sim](/wiki/Alastair_Sim "Alastair Sim") and Sir [Sean Connery](/wiki/Sean_Connery "Sean Connery"), the first cinematic [James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond"),[[290]](#cite_note-296) the comedian and actor [Ronnie Corbett](/wiki/Ronnie_Corbett "Ronnie Corbett"), one of [The Two Ronnies](/wiki/The_Two_Ronnies "The Two Ronnies"),[[291]](#cite_note-297) and the impressionist [Rory Bremner](/wiki/Rory_Bremner "Rory Bremner"). Artists from the city include the portrait painters Sir [Henry Raeburn](/wiki/Henry_Raeburn "Henry Raeburn"), Sir [David Wilkie](/wiki/David_Wilkie_(artist) "David Wilkie (artist)"), and [Allan Ramsay](/wiki/Allan_Ramsay_(artist) "Allan Ramsay (artist)"). The city has produced or been home to musicians [Ian Anderson](/wiki/Ian_Anderson "Ian Anderson"), front man of the band [Jethro Tull](/wiki/Jethro_Tull_(band) "Jethro Tull (band)"), [The Incredible String Band](/wiki/The_Incredible_String_Band "The Incredible String Band"), the folk duo [The Corries](/wiki/The_Corries "The Corries"), [Wattie Buchan](/wiki/Wattie_Buchan "Wattie Buchan"), lead singer and founding member of punk band [The Exploited](/wiki/The_Exploited "The Exploited"), [Shirley Manson](/wiki/Shirley_Manson "Shirley Manson"), lead singer of the band [Garbage](/wiki/Garbage_(band) "Garbage (band)"), the [Bay City Rollers](/wiki/Bay_City_Rollers "Bay City Rollers"), [The Proclaimers](/wiki/The_Proclaimers "The Proclaimers"), [Swim School](/wiki/Swim_School "Swim School"), [Boards of Canada](/wiki/Boards_of_Canada "Boards of Canada") and [Idlewild](/wiki/Idlewild_(band) "Idlewild (band)"). Edinburgh is the birthplace of former British prime minister [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") who attended the city's [Fettes College](/wiki/Fettes_College "Fettes College").[[292]](#cite_note-298)

Criminals from Edinburgh's past include [Deacon Brodie](/wiki/Deacon_Brodie "Deacon Brodie"), head of a trades guild and Edinburgh city councillor by day but a burglar by night, who is said to have been the inspiration for [Robert Louis Stevenson](/wiki/Robert_Louis_Stevenson "Robert Louis Stevenson")'s story, the *[Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde](/wiki/Strange_Case_of_Dr_Jekyll_and_Mr_Hyde "Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde")*,[[293]](#cite_note-299) and murderers [Burke and Hare](/wiki/Burke_and_Hare "Burke and Hare") who delivered fresh corpses for dissection to the famous anatomist [Robert Knox](/wiki/Robert_Knox_(surgeon) "Robert Knox (surgeon)").[[294]](#cite_note-300)

Another Edinburgh resident was [Greyfriars Bobby](/wiki/Greyfriars_Bobby "Greyfriars Bobby"). The small [Skye Terrier](/wiki/Skye_Terrier "Skye Terrier") reputedly kept vigil over his dead master's grave in [Greyfriars Kirkyard](/wiki/Greyfriars_Kirkyard "Greyfriars Kirkyard") for 14 years in the 1860s and 1870s, giving rise to a story of canine devotion which plays a part in attracting visitors to the city.[[295]](#cite_note-301)

## International relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: International relations")]

### Twin towns and sister cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Edinburgh&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Twin towns and sister cities")]

See also: [List of twin towns and sister cities in Scotland](/wiki/List_of_twin_towns_and_sister_cities_in_Scotland "List of twin towns and sister cities in Scotland")

The City of Edinburgh has entered into 14 international [twinning](/wiki/Town_twinning "Town twinning") arrangements since 1954.[[296]](#cite_note-Edinburgh_twinning-302) Most of the arrangements are styled as *Twin Cities* but the agreement with [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków") is designated as a *Partner City*,[[296]](#cite_note-Edinburgh_twinning-302) and the agreement with [Kyoto Prefecture](/wiki/Kyoto_Prefecture "Kyoto Prefecture") is officially styled as a *Friendship Link*, reflecting its status as the only region to be twinned with Edinburgh.[[296]](#cite_note-Edinburgh_twinning-302)

In June 2024, the City of Edinburgh Council shelved plans for a friendship arrangement with [Kaohsiung](/wiki/Kaohsiung "Kaohsiung"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan"), after a report raised concerns that the agreement could heighten cyber attacks. A few weeks before the decision, the Chinese consul general met with Scottish government minister [Angus Robertson](/wiki/Angus_Robertson "Angus Robertson") to protest against the potential agreement. In a letter to the city council, the Chinese representative said signing a sister city agreement "will [hurt the feeling of the Chinese people](/wiki/Hurting_the_feelings_of_the_Chinese_people "Hurting the feelings of the Chinese people") and bring about serious consequences to … bilateral relations".[[297]](#cite_note-303)

| --- | --- |

| [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice"), France | 1958[[298]](#cite_note-Nice_Jumellage-304)[[299]](#cite_note-Archant_twinning-305) |
| [Florence](/wiki/Florence "Florence"), Italy | 1964 |
| [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin"), New Zealand | 1974 |
| [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), British Columbia, Canada | 1977[[300]](#cite_note-Vancouver-306) |
| [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"), California, United States | 1977 |
| [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an"), China | 1985 |
| [Segovia](/wiki/Segovia "Segovia"), Spain | 1985[[301]](#cite_note-Edinburgh-307) |
| [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv"), Ukraine | 1989 |
| [Aalborg](/wiki/Aalborg "Aalborg"), Denmark | 1991[[302]](#cite_note-Aalborg_twinnings-308) |
| [Kyoto Prefecture](/wiki/Kyoto_Prefecture "Kyoto Prefecture"), Japan | 1994 |
| [Kathmandu](/wiki/Kathmandu "Kathmandu"), [Nepal](/wiki/Nepal "Nepal") | 1994 |
| [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), Poland | 1995[[303]](#cite_note-Kraków_partnerships-309) |
